SAP Portfolio Briefing | May 2026 · FY2026 |
YTD Consumed $39.0M YTD Budget $44.6M YTD ACV $50.7M | Proj Consumed $93.7M Proj Budget $108.3M Proj ACV $121.6M
AbbVie Inc
Life sciences and healthcare
YTD Consumed: $1.4M
YTD Budget: $1.8M
YTD ACV: $1.9M
Projected Consumed: $3.3M
Projected Budget: $4.5M
Projected ACV: $4.6M
EA Insights and Actions
AbbVie has contracted a full BTP Enterprise Agreement (~$82,809 budget across Joule Studio, Integration Suite, Technology Foundation, plus Database/Public Cloud tenants) that sits at $0 consumed while SAC BI, SAC Planning, Concur, and Ariba all run as independent silos — the integration and AI backbone meant to unify Finance, Supply Chain, and Analytics is paid for but architecturally absent, blocking any closed-loop story between expense data, supplier governance, serialization events, and enterprise reporting.
Every L1 domain shows the same inverted pattern — transactional/visualization layers are running hot (T&E at 115–140%, Ariba Buying at 100%+, SAC BI live, Traceability Hub active) while their governance, planning, and extensibility counterparts (Supplier Lifecycle at 81% declining, Batch Release Hub dormant, SAC Planning at 55%, BTP at 0%, SSO dormant) are starving — meaning AbbVie is scaling execution velocity without the upstream control plane, which for a GxP-regulated pharma directly threatens DSCSA/EU FMD compliance posture and supplier qualification integrity.
Identity and integration fabric is the silent single point of failure — Single Sign-on is fully dormant and Integration Suite has zero consumption, meaning Concur, Ariba, SAC, and Traceability Hub are operating as disconnected identity and data islands, blocking the unified employee-to-supplier-to-product traceability that AbbVie's compliance and finance close processes ultimately depend on.
Convene an enterprise architecture review binding the dormant BTP Enterprise Agreement to one anchor workload per L1 domain — Integration Suite for Concur-Ariba-SAC identity and data flow, Joule Studio for Supplier Lifecycle reactivation, and Technology Foundation for Traceability-to-Batch Release Hub orchestration — to convert $82,809 of shelfware into the connective tissue across Finance, Supply Chain, and Analytics.
Run a pharma compliance architecture workshop pairing Traceability Hub's ~$575K unconsumed serialization capacity with the fully dormant Batch Release Hub to scope an end-to-end GxP Make-and-Release chain covering SKU/trade-lane onboarding and electronic batch disposition before regulatory exposure compounds.
Launch a cross-domain governance and activation session covering Single Sign-on, SAC Planning expansion, and Ariba Supplier Lifecycle re-engagement to close the identity, planning, and supplier-governance gaps that are simultaneously eroding controls across T&E, P2P, and FP&A.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
48.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$160K
YTD Budget$332K
YTD ACV: $334K
Projected Consumed: $385K · Projected Budget: $839K · Projected ACV: $802K
Solution Insights and Actions
AbbVie's Data & AI architecture is inverted — SAC BI is live but capped at a single reporting use case while the BTP Enterprise Agreement that should underpin it with integration, data services, and AI extensibility sits at zero consumption across both Public and Database Cloud entitlements, meaning the LoB has visualization without the platform foundation to enrich, extend, or operationalize those insights.
SAC BI's flat ~45% attainment ($160K YTD vs $331K budget, pacing to ~$413K shelfware against the $798K annual) reflects a closed analytics footprint rather than an enterprise BI capability — a fixed user group is running a fixed report set, leaving commercial, supply chain, and R&D reporting domains architecturally unserved despite the entitlement being paid for.
Run a SAC BI use-case and user activation workshop to expand dashboard domains into commercial, supply chain, and R&D reporting and convert dormant licensed personas into active consumers, directly attacking the ~$36K/month budget gap.
Activate the BTP Enterprise Agreement (both Public and Database Cloud tenants, ~$40K combined) by binding Integration Suite or Data Services to a named SAC BI data pipeline, turning the dormant platform entitlement into the architectural backbone that feeds and extends AbbVie's analytics layer.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
19.9%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$109
YTD Budget$547
YTD ACV: $405
Projected Consumed: $261 · Projected Budget: $84K · Projected ACV: $971
Solution Insights and Actions
AbbVie's entire BTP Enterprise Agreement is architecturally provisioned across Joule Studio, Integration Suite, and broader Technology Foundation services but operationally inert — with $0 consumed against a combined $82,809 budget, the LoB has contracted for an agentic AI, integration, and extension backbone that no business process is currently leveraging, leaving every downstream SAP extension and integration scenario without its intended foundation.
SAP Build is technically live but strategically stalled — a single $54 pilot in Jan–Feb followed by three consecutive zero-consumption months indicates the low-code capability exists in the landscape but has no citizen developer program or app pipeline behind it, meaning AbbVie owns the extensibility tooling for BTP without the demand engine to feed it, which also explains why the surrounding BTP agreement has nothing to integrate or extend.
Run a unified BTP activation workshop spanning Integration Suite, Joule Studio, and Technology Foundation to identify the first production workload and convert the $82,809 dormant Enterprise Agreement into live consumption anchored to a concrete LoB use case.
Re-engage the original SAP Build pilot sponsors to diagnose the post-February stall and stand up a citizen developer pipeline of 3–5 net-new apps or workflows that pull through the dormant BTP services as their integration and AI backbone.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
97.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$1.1M
YTD Budget$1.1M
YTD ACV: $1.3M
Projected Consumed: $2.6M · Projected Budget: $2.7M · Projected ACV: $3.2M
Solution Insights and Actions
AbbVie's Travel & Expense stack is the architectural center of gravity for Finance and Spend Management — Concur Expense, Direct Travel, Intelligent Audit, Reporting Services, ExpenseIt, Triplink, Company Bill Statement, Connectors, Preferred Success, and WalkMe are all running 115–140% attainment in unison, meaning the entire T&E value chain (capture, booking, audit, reporting, enablement) is operating beyond its contracted envelope and will hit ACV ceilings as a system, not as isolated products, risking coordinated service throttling across the LoB's expense operations.
The Procure-to-Pay process is architecturally contracted but governance is eroding — Ariba Buying and Invoicing is running hot at 100%+ attainment while Ariba Supplier Lifecycle & Performance Management has flipped to 81% and is trending down, meaning AbbVie is transacting purchases and invoices at full velocity while the upstream supplier qualification, segmentation, and performance discipline that should feed those transactions is weakening — a classic pattern where compliant buying outpaces supplier governance.
The planning and platform foundation is structurally underutilized — SAC Planning is plateaued at ~55% attainment serving a fixed FP&A use case while the SAP BTP Enterprise Agreement sits at $0 consumed and Single Sign-on is fully dormant, meaning the extensibility, integration, and identity layer that would let FP&A expand SAC into workforce/OpEx/sales planning and securely connect Concur and Ariba identities is contracted but architecturally absent.
Initiate a coordinated T&E true-up across Concur Expense, Direct Travel, Intelligent Audit, Reporting Services, ExpenseIt, Triplink, Company Bill Statement, Connectors & Extract Services, Preferred Success, and WalkMe to right-size the full expense value chain against the demonstrated 115–140% attainment pattern rather than negotiating each SKU in isolation.
Convene a P2P governance review pairing the Ariba Buying and Invoicing volume signal with the declining Ariba Supplier Lifecycle & Performance Management consumption to re-activate stalled supplier qualification, segmentation, and scorecard workflows so transactional buying is backed by governed supplier data.
Launch a joint activation workshop covering SAP BTP Enterprise Agreement, Single Sign-on, and SAC Planning expansion — committing a first BTP production workload, completing IdP integration for Concur and Ariba identities, and extending SAC beyond its current FP&A plateau into adjacent planning domains.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
32.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$122K
YTD Budget$379K
YTD ACV: $269K
Projected Consumed: $292K · Projected Budget: $867K · Projected ACV: $645K
Solution Insights and Actions
AbbVie's Make & Deliver compliance architecture is structurally incomplete — Traceability Hub is live but running at ~34% of contracted capacity while Batch Release Hub sits fully dormant, meaning serialization is partially active but the GxP electronic batch release process it should feed into is still paper-bound, breaking the intended end-to-end pharma compliance chain.
Traceability Hub's May -$40,034 reversal against a previously stable ~$40K monthly run-rate indicates a billing or data correction event rather than a usage decline, but architecturally the bigger issue is that ~$575K of unconsumed serialization capacity points to entire SKU families, markets, or DSCSA/EU FMD trade lanes that have never been onboarded to the traceability backbone.
Activate Batch Release Hub by scoping a first batch disposition workflow with AbbVie's quality and manufacturing teams, covering tenant provisioning, master data setup, and release decision dashboard configuration to convert the $480 dormant contract into live GxP consumption.
Run a Traceability Hub expansion workshop with AbbVie's serialization leads to onboard additional SKUs and regulatory trade lanes against the ~$575K unconsumed budget, and in parallel reconcile the May -$40,034 anomaly with billing to restore a clean YTD adoption baseline.
Abbott Laboratories
Life sciences and healthcare
YTD Consumed: $1.5M
YTD Budget: $1.8M
YTD ACV: $2.1M
Projected Consumed: $3.7M
Projected Budget: $4.3M
Projected ACV: $5.1M
EA Insights and Actions
Abbott has invested in the connectivity backbone (Integration Suite at 107%+) and the data ingestion layer (Data Intelligence Cloud at 99%) but the strategic persistence and extension tiers — HANA Cloud at 65% and SAP Build at 40% — are dormant, meaning data is moving and being visualized but never landing in the contracted business data fabric or being turned into differentiated applications, fragmenting the Business Data Cloud + BTP architecture Abbott is paying ~$2M+ annually to operate.
Across Procurement (Ariba Buying 55.8%, Supplier Risk 21.3%), Customer Experience (C4C dormant), and Planning (SAC Planning 63–70%), the execution and governance layers consistently underperform while the upstream design layers (Sourcing, Incentive Management, IBP) run at design intent — Abbott is architecting decisions well but lacks the closed-loop execution discipline to convert them into compliant spend, served customers, and integrated plans, which is the exact closed-loop life-sciences operating model the SAP stack was contracted to enable.
IBP is the sole active pillar of Supply Chain running at zero-headroom against a $1.48M ceiling with no execution-side counterpart, and it is not integrated with the underutilized HANA Cloud or the dormant SAC Planning workforce/capex models — meaning Abbott's regulated-manufacturing supply plans cannot be stress-tested against financial or analytical scenarios, leaving the LoB exposed precisely where pharma/medical-device traceability and resilience mandates demand integrated planning.
Convene a Business Data Cloud architecture review across Data & AI and Technology Foundation to redirect Data Intelligence Cloud pipelines and SAC BI workloads onto the underutilized HANA Cloud foundation and pair it with a BTP/SAP Build enablement sprint that monetizes the over-consumed Integration Suite iFlows into citizen-developer extensions.
Run a closed-loop execution workshop spanning Finance & Spend (Ariba Buying, Supplier Risk), Customer Experience (C4C activation), and Finance (SAC Planning extension into workforce/capex) to convert the upstream design-layer maturity into downstream transactional, governance, and integrated-planning outcomes.
Initiate an integrated supply-chain-to-finance roadmap session anchored on IBP capacity uplift, mapping dormant IBP modules (demand sensing, inventory optimization) to HANA Cloud persistence and SAC Planning financial scenarios so Abbott secures contractual headroom while building the multi-pillar SCM architecture its regulated operations require.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
74.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$66K
YTD Budget$89K
YTD ACV: $113K
Projected Consumed: $159K · Projected Budget: $219K · Projected ACV: $272K
Solution Insights and Actions
Abbott's Data & AI stack is architecturally complete for ingest-to-visualization — Data Intelligence Cloud pipelines feed HANA Cloud which surfaces through SAC BI — but the middle tier is hollow: HANA Cloud is consuming at only 65% of plan while SAC BI is straining 15% above ceiling, meaning the analytics layer is being pushed hard against a database foundation that is materially underutilized.
The business data fabric role HANA Cloud is contracted to play — anchoring AI, Datasphere integration, and best-of-breed compute — is largely dormant with $63K of projected unused capacity, while Data Intelligence Cloud's near-perfect 99% attainment shows pipelines are landing data somewhere other than the strategic HANA foundation, fragmenting the intended Business Data Cloud architecture.
Redirect SAP Analytics Cloud BI's overconsumption pressure onto SAP HANA Cloud by migrating the Q1 BI workloads driving the $4,153 projected overage onto the underutilized HANA instance, closing the $23,659 YTD HANA gap while right-sizing SAC BI entitlement to Abbott's demonstrated burn rate.
Connect Data Intelligence Cloud pipelines to SAP HANA Cloud as the persistence and AI anchor layer — using the $478 of DIC headroom to onboard an adjacent Abbott business unit's data flows into HANA — so the contracted business data fabric actually carries the workloads it was provisioned for.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
106.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$137K
YTD Budget$129K
YTD ACV: $135K
Projected Consumed: $328K · Projected Budget: $286K · Projected ACV: $323K
Solution Insights and Actions
The integration backbone is being consumed faster than contracted while the low-code extension layer is dormant — Integration Suite is running at 107%+ attainment delivering APIs, iFlows, and AI-ready connectivity, but SAP Build sits at ~40% attainment, meaning Abbott is moving data between systems but not building the apps, workflows, or process extensions that would turn those integrations into business outcomes.
The Technology Foundation is operating as a pure integration platform rather than an application platform — heavy Integration Suite burn ($326K projected vs $282K budget) without corresponding SAP Build adoption ($1,468 projected vs $3,352 budget) indicates Abbott has invested in connectivity but has no citizen developer motion to capitalize on it, leaving custom extensions, automations, and Joule Studio-driven agent scenarios unrealized.
Open a true-up on SAP Integration Suite to size contracted capacity against the $44,341 projected overrun and the May ACV step-up trajectory, ensuring the integration backbone has runway before ACV is exhausted.
Pair an SAP Build enablement sprint with the active Integration Suite workloads — anchor 3–5 low-code app or workflow use cases on top of the iFlows already in production so the $935 YTD Build budget gap converts into extensions that monetize the integration investment.
Solution Area
Customer Experience
59.8%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$198K
YTD Budget$332K
YTD ACV: $384K
Projected Consumed: $476K · Projected Budget: $799K · Projected ACV: $923K
Solution Insights and Actions
Abbott's Customer Experience architecture splits across engagement (Cloud for Customer) and monetization (SAP Incentive Management), but only the incentive layer was operating at design intent until May — the front-office CRM is structurally deployed yet functionally under-leveraged, meaning sales and service teams have the system of engagement without fully exercising pipeline, service ticketing, or mobile capabilities that justify the contracted footprint.
The May -$51,754 reversal in SAP Incentive Management against four months of 98–102% attainment indicates the compensation engine was in healthy production use driving payee calculations and quota tracking, so the anomaly is almost certainly a credit or cycle adjustment rather than abandonment — but until reconciled, the auditable comp ledger that finance and sales leaders depend on is compromised.
Reconcile the -$51,754 May entry in SAP Incentive Management with finance to confirm it is a billing or commission-cycle adjustment, then re-baseline the remaining $252,534 budget against active payee populations to restore the steady attainment pattern.
Launch a Cloud for Customer activation play targeting dormant sales pipeline, service ticketing, and offline mobile modules to lift monthly consumption above the $13,800 budget line and close the projected $70,500 annual shortfall.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
77.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$516K
YTD Budget$670K
YTD ACV: $883K
Projected Consumed: $1.2M · Projected Budget: $1.6M · Projected ACV: $2.1M
Solution Insights and Actions
Abbott's Procure-to-Pay architecture is structurally unbalanced — Ariba Sourcing runs at a healthy 86.8% attainment and Preferred Success advisory is over-consumed at 120%+, yet the downstream Buying and Invoicing layer where sourced agreements convert to transactions is regressing to 55.8%, meaning negotiated savings are being designed upstream but leaking out through unguided buying channels.
The supplier risk control plane is effectively dormant at 21.3% attainment while sourcing and buying are active, leaving Abbott awarding spend and issuing POs without the continuous financial, ESG, and geopolitical monitoring that Ariba Supplier Risk is architected to embed into those same decisions — a governance gap that grows with every sourcing event.
SAP Analytics Cloud Planning is operating as a finance-core point tool rather than the unified financial-and-operational planning layer it is architected to be, with 70.6% YTD attainment and a May drop to 63.1% signaling that workforce, capex, and operational planning models that would extend its reach across the LoB have not been activated.
Preferred Success Ariba is consuming 27% above plan while the procurement modules it is meant to accelerate (Buying, Supplier Risk) underperform, indicating advisory hours are being absorbed without translating into module-level adoption lift — success services are funding activity, not outcomes.
Close the P2P execution gap on Ariba Buying and Invoicing by running a catalog and guided-buying enablement sprint that routes non-catalog spend through compliant channels and reverses the May regression to 55.8%.
Activate Ariba Supplier Risk beyond its current static user group by onboarding additional supplier segments and turning on risk exposure scoring, due diligence workflows, and continuous monitoring to bring the ~$700 monthly run rate toward the ~$3,300 budget target.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
100.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$618K
YTD Budget$618K
YTD ACV: $618K
Projected Consumed: $1.5M · Projected Budget: $1.5M · Projected ACV: $1.5M
Solution Insights and Actions
IBP is the sole active pillar of Abbott's Supply Chain Management architecture, meaning demand sensing, S&OP, inventory optimization, and supply response are all converging onto a single contracted ceiling — the LoB has unified planning but no architectural redundancy or adjacent execution layer to absorb growth.
The perfectly linear $123,616 monthly burn against a $1,483,398 annual ceiling signals IBP has moved past ramp-up into steady-state operationalization, but the zero-headroom trajectory means any expansion into demand sensing or inventory optimization modules will breach contract before delivering incremental planning value.
Initiate an IBP (LPR318) capacity uplift conversation anchored on the proven 100% monthly attainment pattern to secure headroom before mid-year planning cycles trigger overage.
Audit which IBP modules are driving the $123,616 monthly consumption and map dormant capabilities (demand sensing, inventory optimization, supply response) to position a module expansion tied to the contract uplift.
Border States Industries, Inc.
Wholesale distribution
YTD Consumed: $410K
YTD Budget: $454K
YTD ACV: $399K
Projected Consumed: $984K
Projected Budget: $1.1M
Projected ACV: $957K
EA Insights and Actions
Border States has BTP CPEA workloads structurally over-consuming in Data & AI (113%), Joule Studio (208%), and Integration Suite (114%) while the business domains that should ride those rails — Commerce Cloud at 32% attainment, SuccessFactors Compensation at 0%, Career Development at 41% — sit dormant, meaning the platform layer is being paid for and burned through on generic and AI-extensibility workloads that are not yet wired into the customer-facing and talent value chains they were architected to power.
The integration backbone is simultaneously the fastest-growing cost center (Integration Suite + Joule Studio tracking ~$139K run-rate against a pilot-sized contract) and the most likely root cause of the Concur Expense May cliff and the broken hire-to-reward chain in HCM, exposing Border States to a single-point-of-failure pattern where one under-governed integration platform underpins finance close, T&E automation, and HR-to-payroll flows.
Across every L1 the same architectural signature repeats — a working foundational layer (Employee Central, Concur core capture, CPEA, Commerce baseline) with dormant value-realization modules above it — indicating Border States has bought a complete SAP enterprise architecture but operates it as disconnected systems of record, capping ROI on roughly $260K+ of annual contracted capacity that is either over-running on plumbing or under-running on outcomes.
Convene a cross-domain BTP governance and true-up council covering Data & AI, Technology Foundation, Finance, and HCM to right-size the CPEA against the combined Joule/Integration Suite overrun and redirect the Data & AI underrun toward Datasphere/SAC workloads that feed Commerce, Concur, and SuccessFactors analytics.
Run an integration-chain assurance review on Integration Suite spanning Concur Expense→ERP, SuccessFactors EC→Payroll→Compensation, and Commerce→Order Management to diagnose the May Concur Expense collapse and certify the pipes before activating dormant talent and commerce modules.
Launch an enterprise value-realization roadmap workshop that sequences activation of SF Compensation, SF Career Development, Commerce B2B self-service flows, and Data & AI services against the ~$120K+ aggregate annual shortfall, converting contracted but unused capacity into measurable distribution-industry outcomes in margin, talent retention, and digital order share.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
93.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$81K
YTD Budget$87K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $195K · Projected Budget: $216K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is operating as the foundational BTP consumption vehicle for Data & AI workloads at Border States, and five consecutive months of steady $16K+ spend confirms it is anchoring production workloads rather than experimentation — but as the sole adopted product in this LoB, it signals the platform is being consumed generically without visible uptake of higher-value Data & AI services (Datasphere, SAC, AI Foundation) that the agreement is architected to enable.
The recurring overshoot pattern (Jan 110.6%, Apr 101.7%, May 113.7%) against a projected $20,351 annual underrun indicates workload demand is real and growing in peak months, yet capacity planning is reactive — the LoB has committed credits it is not pacing effectively, exposing it to both stranded spend and in-month throttling risk.
Run a Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement workload decomposition with Border States Data & AI stakeholders to map current consumption to specific BTP services and identify whether Datasphere, SAC, or AI capabilities should absorb the $20,351 projected underrun.
Institute a burn-rate governance cadence on the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement that flags any month exceeding 110% attainment and rebalances workload scheduling against the $17,999 average remaining monthly budget to prevent peak-month overage.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
139.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$58K
YTD Budget$42K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $139K · Projected Budget: $102K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
The Technology Foundation platform is architecturally healthy but financially under-provisioned — both Joule Studio and SAP Integration Suite workloads on the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement are consuming at 186–222% of plan, indicating the LoB has operationalized AI extensibility and integration as core capabilities but the contract was sized for pilot-scale, not production-scale, adoption.
Joule Studio consumption running at 208% of annual budget alongside Integration Suite at 114% signals that AI agent and extension workloads are the dominant growth vector on the platform — the LoB is shifting from traditional integration patterns toward AI-driven orchestration, but without consumption governance this pull will breach the annual ceiling and risk service disruption to the very integrations that underpin downstream business processes.
Initiate a CPEA true-up for LPR868 sized against the combined $139K projected run rate across Joule Studio and Integration Suite to prevent ceiling breach and lock in committed-use economics before further overage accrues.
Conduct a BTP workload governance review on LPR868 to classify Joule Studio and Integration Suite consumption between sanctioned production workloads and uncontrolled developer sprawl, and establish tagging and quota controls before expanding contracted capacity.
Solution Area
Customer Experience
32.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$17K
YTD Budget$54K
YTD ACV: $94K
Projected Consumed: $42K · Projected Budget: $132K · Projected ACV: $225K
Solution Insights and Actions
Commerce Cloud is architecturally positioned as Border States' enterprise digital commerce backbone for B2B catalog, self-service, and order management, but the flat ~$3,500/month consumption pattern shows only a narrow baseline workload is live — the platform is functioning as a static storefront rather than the unified omnichannel engine it is designed to be.
The capabilities meant to drive incremental value — B2B2C storefronts, personalized catalogs, promotions, and AI-driven commerce innovation — are dormant, leaving Border States with enterprise-grade commerce licensing supporting a departmental use case and tracking to only ~32% of its $131,635 annual plan.
Run a Commerce Cloud use-case expansion workshop with Border States to activate at least one additional B2B self-service flow, customer segment, or storefront against the $15,172 monthly ACV gap.
Conduct a Commerce Cloud consumption driver review to map the flat $3,500/month baseline to specific transactions and identify which catalog, order management, or personalization capabilities can be switched on to close the $90,072 shortfall.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
116.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$67K
YTD Budget$57K
YTD ACV: $68K
Projected Consumed: $160K · Projected Budget: $137K · Projected ACV: $162K
Solution Insights and Actions
Border States' Travel and Expense architecture is fully wired end-to-end — capture (ExpenseIt), submission (Expense), policy enforcement (Intelligent Audit), payment automation (Expense Pay), and analytics (Reporting Services) are all live — but four of the five components are running 27–37% above contracted capacity, meaning the LoB is extracting real value from an integrated T&E stack that is now structurally under-sized for actual business volume.
The May collapse in Concur Expense to $507 while Expense Pay, ExpenseIt, Intelligent Audit, and Reporting Services continue at normal run-rates is architecturally inconsistent — the upstream submission engine cannot be dormant while downstream audit, payment, and reporting layers remain active, indicating a likely broken integration feed or reporting artifact rather than a genuine business stoppage that needs urgent diagnosis before it cascades into the dependent Concur modules.
Diagnose the Concur Expense May cliff ($6,159 April to $507 May) by tracing whether expense submission, approver workflow, or ERP integration feeds have broken, since downstream Expense Pay, Intelligent Audit, and ExpenseIt activity contradicts a true usage halt.
Initiate a consolidated true-up across Concur Expense, ExpenseIt, Intelligent Audit, and Reporting Services to realign roughly $33K of combined annual budget shortfall with the projected run-rate driven by embedded, structurally over-plan T&E adoption.
Solution Area
Human Capital Management
87.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$187K
YTD Budget$214K
YTD ACV: $237K
Projected Consumed: $448K · Projected Budget: $517K · Projected ACV: $570K
Solution Insights and Actions
Border States has built a functioning HR system of record — Employee Central and Employee Central Payroll are both burning at near-100% attainment — meaning the foundational data layer for talent, compensation, and development is live and trustworthy, yet the talent modules that should consume that data (Career Development at 41%, Compensation at 0%) are starved, leaving the LoB with a fully operational core HR backbone feeding a dormant talent architecture.
The hire-to-grow-to-reward value chain is architecturally contracted but operationally broken — Recruiting and Onboarding are running at fixed, narrow volumes (~$6,550 and ~$1,050/month), Performance and Goals is capped at a stable user base, Career Development is stagnant, and Compensation has never activated — so new hires enter through a working funnel but never reach the performance, development, and pay-for-performance workflows that justify the SuccessFactors talent suite investment.
Activate SF Compensation by anchoring it to a live merit or bonus cycle using Employee Central salary data and Performance and Goals review outputs, closing the architectural break where approved pay decisions cannot flow to payroll.
Launch a talent-suite activation play that extends the proven Employee Central user base into SF Career and Talent Development, SF Performance and Goals, and SF Onboarding through skills profiles, development goals, and continuous feedback workflows to convert the dormant ~$30K combined annual gap into realized talent outcomes.
CHS Inc.
Agribusiness
YTD Consumed: $1.6M
YTD Budget: $2.1M
YTD ACV: $2.3M
Projected Consumed: $3.9M
Projected Budget: $5.2M
Projected ACV: $5.4M
EA Insights and Actions
CHS has contracted a BTP-anchored platform spine across Data & AI, Technology Foundation, and Finance, yet Integration Suite is the only pool burning hot (125%) while Datasphere, Joule analytics, SAC Planning, and a dedicated Technology Foundation entitlement sit dormant or under-consumed — meaning the enterprise is moving data between systems but not converging it into the unified planning, AI, and extension fabric that justifies the platform bet, leaving every downstream domain (Finance, HCM, Supply Chain) dependent on point integrations rather than a coherent data and intelligence layer.
The transactional systems of record — Employee Central in HCM and IBP in Supply Chain — are healthy, but every value-multiplier layer above them (Performance→Compensation, Recruiting, IBP Demand/Supply/Control Tower, Signavio process governance, SAC Planning) is eroding or dormant, indicating CHS's architecture is stuck at 'system of record' maturity and is structurally blocked from delivering the cross-functional planning, talent, and process-transformation outcomes its agribusiness margin pressure demands.
Across the portfolio CHS shows a consistent contract-ahead-of-consumption pattern — Signavio (~31% YTD, ~$50K shortfall), SAC Planning (7.6% of $799K), Fieldglass (34%/24% attainment, ~$222K gap), IBP ($93K shortfall), and a zero-burn Technology Foundation — signaling that commercial expansion has outpaced an enterprise activation and governance model, with roughly $400K+ of annual capacity at risk of forfeiture and the corresponding business capabilities (process transformation, FP&A, contingent workforce governance, integrated planning) remaining unrealized.
Convene a BTP architecture review across Data & AI, Finance, and Technology Foundation to redirect the projected BTP Public under-burn and dormant Technology Foundation entitlement into Datasphere, SAC Planning, and Joule workloads that industrialize the over-consumed SAC BI and Integration Suite surfaces into a unified data-and-planning fabric.
Run a cross-domain 'system-of-record to value-layer' activation workshop spanning HCM (Performance→Compensation→Recruiting), Supply Chain (IBP dormant modules), and Finance (Fieldglass reinstatement) to restore the integration chains that turn EC and IBP foundations into closed-loop talent and supply outcomes.
Establish an enterprise consumption governance board to true-up the SAC BI and Integration Suite overages against the Signavio, SAC Planning, Fieldglass, IBP, and Technology Foundation shortfalls, converting at-risk ACV into named activation workloads with assigned business owners before forfeiture compounds.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
110.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$321K
YTD Budget$290K
YTD ACV: $96K
Projected Consumed: $770K · Projected Budget: $773K · Projected ACV: $230K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI footprint at CHS is architecturally bifurcated — BTP (Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement) is delivering the runtime and data services while SAC BI is the consumption layer, and both are now in active operational use rather than pilot, but the catalog's planning and Joule-driven AI capabilities of SAC remain unevidenced, meaning CHS is extracting visualization value without the unified analytics-plus-planning outcome the platform is designed to deliver.
SAC BI is consistently over-consuming (112.5% YTD, projected $22,578 overage) while BTP Public is trending to a $27,905 under-burn — the LoB is scaling the analytics consumption surface faster than the underlying BTP data and integration services that should feed it, indicating dashboards are being driven from narrow or siloed data pipelines rather than a broadening BTP-anchored data fabric.
Initiate a SAC BI true-up to close the projected $22,578 overage and, in the same motion, map the high-consumption dashboards back to BTP data services to redirect the $27,905 BTP shortfall into integration, Datasphere, and AI services that industrialize those analytics use cases.
Run a Data & AI capability activation review to extend CHS beyond BI into SAC planning and Joule-based analytics, converting the dormant unified planning capability into an active LoB workload that justifies both the SAC expansion and the BTP back-half ramp.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
106.7%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$112K
YTD Budget$105K
YTD ACV: $1K
Projected Consumed: $268K · Projected Budget: $248K · Projected ACV: $3K
Solution Insights and Actions
CHS Inc.'s Technology Foundation is architecturally bifurcated — the BTP enterprise agreement is heavily consumed under Integration Suite (125% attainment, $186K projected vs $163K budget) and healthy under Joule Studio, while a separate Technology Foundation allocation sits at zero, meaning integration and AI orchestration are live production capabilities but a budgeted foundation workload has never been activated and risks full forfeiture.
SAP Build is the only low-code extensibility lever in this footprint and it is eroding month-over-month ($132→$114) even as YTD consumption already exceeds budget — the LoB has the platform to extend SAP processes with custom apps and workflows, but declining builder activity signals that citizen development is losing momentum just as Integration Suite workloads expand, leaving integrations without a matching app/extension layer to surface them to business users.
Run a Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement workload governance and true-up review across the Integration Suite and Joule Studio pools to right-size the combined ~$26K projected overrun before unbudgeted overage charges hit.
Launch a Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement activation workshop for the dormant Technology Foundation entitlement to identify a first workload and convert the $5,960 annual budget into live consumption rather than forfeiture.
Solution Area
Business Transformation Management
30.8%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$9K
YTD Budget$30K
YTD ACV: $94K
Projected Consumed: $22K · Projected Budget: $73K · Projected ACV: $225K
Solution Insights and Actions
Business Transformation Management at CHS is architecturally anchored on a single product — SAP Signavio Process Management — and that anchor is dormant, meaning the LoB has the contractual foundation for process discovery, modeling, and governance but no operational capability to drive transformation decisions from it.
The inverse pattern of a 5x ACV expansion against a five-month consumption decline (from $2,821 to $993, with YTD at ~31% of budget) indicates the contract scaled ahead of an enablement model, leaving CHS paying for transformation capacity it cannot currently consume and projecting a ~$50K shortfall against the $73,065 annual plan.
Stand up a Signavio Process Management activation sprint anchored on one high-value end-to-end process to reverse the consumption decline and establish a repeatable modeling practice that justifies the expanded ACV.
Conduct a license utilization and seat assignment review on Signavio Process Management to confirm whether the May ACV jump to $46,854 maps to deployed modelers, and reassign or onboard users to close the $45,861 ACV-to-consumption gap.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
34.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$200K
YTD Budget$578K
YTD ACV: $902K
Projected Consumed: $479K · Projected Budget: $1.5M · Projected ACV: $2.2M
Solution Insights and Actions
CHS's external workforce architecture is collapsing on both axes — Fieldglass Contingent Workforce and Services Procurement are simultaneously eroding (attainment at 34% and 24% respectively), meaning the LoB has lost its VMS system-of-record for both staff augmentation and SOW spend, and contingent labor is now flowing outside the governed Ariba/SuccessFactors integration with no spend visibility.
The FP&A stack is architecturally provisioned but functionally dormant — SAC Planning is consuming at 7.6% of a $799K budget while the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement has never been activated, meaning CHS owns the planning and extension platform but has not operationalized a single flagship planning model, leaving finance with spreadsheets despite a contracted unified planning capability.
Identity governance is stuck in steady-state — Cloud Identity & Access Governance runs at a flat ~$24.6K monthly baseline while Single Sign-on consumption has collapsed from $116 to $23, indicating CHS has IAG configured for an initial scope but is not expanding access certification or SoD coverage, and the on-premise SSO authentication layer underneath is being abandoned rather than extended.
Reinstate Fieldglass as the mandated system of record for all contingent labor and SOW engagements at CHS by auditing off-platform hires across procurement and HR, onboarding bypassed business units and job categories, and closing the combined ~$222K projected ACV gap across LPR340 and LPR832.
Activate the SAP Analytics Cloud Planning and Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement stack together by selecting a flagship FP&A use case (financial or workforce planning), assigning technical owners to provision the BTP environment, and committing to named planning models with active user targets to convert the dormant $801K combined commitment into recurring consumption.
Solution Area
Human Capital Management
88.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$275K
YTD Budget$311K
YTD ACV: $357K
Projected Consumed: $659K · Projected Budget: $753K · Projected ACV: $857K
Solution Insights and Actions
Employee Central is operating as the healthy system of record with steady upward attainment, but the talent layer built on top of it — Compensation, Performance and Goals, and Recruiting — is eroding in lockstep, meaning the core HR foundation is live while the dependent talent processes that should consume EC's employee and skills data are losing engagement.
The Performance → Compensation architectural chain is breaking down — Performance and Goals consumption is declining 5 points while Compensation attainment slides in parallel, indicating that without active goal-setting and review data feeding merit and bonus worksheets, CHS is running comp cycles on thin performance signal and leaving ~$35K of combined talent value unrealized against budget.
Re-anchor SF Performance and Goals to the Compensation cycle by activating goal cascading, continuous check-ins, and calibration so performance ratings flow into merit worksheets and reverse the joint attainment decline across both modules.
Launch a Recruiting-to-Onboarding-to-EC activation play that lifts SF Recruiting Management above its $10,693 consumption ceiling by turning on interview central, offer approvals, and internal mobility, and connects requisitions through SF Onboarding into Employee Central to close the $8,510 recruiting shortfall.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
88.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$692K
YTD Budget$785K
YTD ACV: $817K
Projected Consumed: $1.7M · Projected Budget: $1.9M · Projected ACV: $2.0M
Solution Insights and Actions
IBP is the sole active pillar of CHS's supply chain planning architecture, and its flat consumption ceiling between $132K and $147K signals the platform is being used as a recurring S&OP calendar tool rather than the unified demand-sensing, inventory optimization, and supply response engine it is designed to be — CHS is paying for an integrated planning suite but operating it as a single-module planning workbook.
The $93,358 cumulative shortfall against an $785,118 YTD budget is structural rather than cyclical, indicating that entire architectural capabilities — likely AI-powered demand sensing, multilevel supply planning, or Control Tower collaboration — are contracted but dormant, leaving CHS without the cross-functional planning convergence that justifies the IBP investment.
Conduct an IBP module activation audit at CHS to map which of Demand, Supply, S&OP, Inventory, and Control Tower are live versus dormant, and stand up the highest-value dormant module to close the $93,358 consumption gap.
Establish an IBP planner enablement and expansion program to break the fixed $132K–$147K consumption baseline by onboarding new planning personas and shifting recurring cycles from monthly to weekly cadence.
CLEVELAND-CLIFFS INC.
Mining
YTD Consumed: $119K
YTD Budget: $162K
YTD ACV: $215K
Projected Consumed: $285K
Projected Budget: $392K
Projected ACV: $516K
EA Insights and Actions
Cleveland-Cliffs has provisioned a multi-domain SAP estate — IBP for planning, C4C for front-office, SAC for analytics, and Integration Suite as the connective tissue — but every domain shows single-workload activation, meaning the customer owns the components of an integrated steel manufacturing operating model while running each as an isolated tool, blocking the order-to-plan-to-cash visibility that defines competitive advantage in commodity steel.
Integration Suite operating as a static $27/month point-to-point bridge against a $1,706 budget is the architectural root cause behind C4C's suspected CRM island, IBP's dormant demand/supply modules, and SAC's BI-only posture — the connective tissue meant to fuse customer signals, planning cadence, and analytics is absent, leaving roughly $88,000+ in combined FY2026 under-consumption across IBP and Integration Suite as direct evidence that the integrated enterprise was contracted but never assembled.
The SAC footprint is confined to descriptive BI on BDC Public with no Joule, planning, or predictive layer activated, while IBP runs episodically and C4C lacks ERP closure — meaning Cleveland-Cliffs has no architectural path to AI-driven margin, mix, or demand decisioning, which is precisely the capability North American integrated steelmakers need to defend against import pricing and decarbonization cost pressure.
Convene a cross-domain integration architecture review spanning Integration Suite, IBP, C4C, and S/4HANA to design the iFlows that close the order-to-plan-to-cash loop and redirect the $1,386 unconsumed Integration Suite budget into governed B2B, EDI, and event-driven scenarios.
Run a joint IBP–SAC–Joule activation workshop to extend SAC from BI into integrated planning bound to IBP's demand and supply modules, converting the flat $19,419 IBP baseline and the unmeasured SAC footprint into a unified analytics-and-planning platform with a named S&OP cadence.
Establish a Cleveland-Cliffs enterprise consumption and entitlement baseline across C4C, SAC, IBP, and Integration Suite to expose shelfware risk where contract insights are absent and align FY2026 spend with a sequenced activation roadmap anchored to steel-industry margin and supply resilience outcomes.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
17.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$242
YTD Budget$1K
YTD ACV: $2K
Projected Consumed: $580 · Projected Budget: $3K · Projected ACV: $6K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI footprint for this LoB consists solely of SAP Analytics Cloud BI on BDC Public, meaning the architecture is configured for visualization and reporting but lacks the connected planning, predictive, and Joule-driven AI layers that SAC is designed to unify — the LoB has a BI tool, not an analytics-and-planning platform.
With no contract insights available for SAC BI, there is no visible commercial signal confirming sustained adoption or scale, which suggests the capability is either in early deployment or running on shared entitlements rather than a committed LoB-owned consumption baseline.
Establish a measurable SAC BI consumption baseline for Cleveland-Cliffs by validating contract entitlements, active user populations, and BDC Public dataset connections to confirm the LoB is realizing the intended BI value.
Extend SAC beyond BI into integrated planning and Joule-based AI scenarios so the LoB moves from descriptive reporting to unified financial and operational decision-making, which is the product's core architectural intent.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
19.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$133
YTD Budget$698
YTD ACV: $1K
Projected Consumed: $320 · Projected Budget: $2K · Projected ACV: $3K
Solution Insights and Actions
Integration Suite is architecturally positioned as the connective tissue for AI agents, APIs, and cross-system data flows, but Cleveland-Cliffs is running it as a single static iFlow — the foundation layer that should be orchestrating S/4HANA, supply chain, and third-party EDI traffic is instead acting as a point-to-point bridge, leaving the broader SAP estate without a governed integration backbone.
With consumption flat at $26–$27/month and projected annual spend of $320 against a $1,706 budget, the $1,386 unconsumed value confirms that planned B2B, API-led, and event-driven scenarios never industrialized — Cleveland-Cliffs is paying for an enterprise integration platform while operating at departmental utility levels, capping the AI and process automation roadmap that depends on it.
Run an Integration Suite use-case discovery workshop with Cleveland-Cliffs' integration, supply chain, and S/4HANA teams to surface net-new iFlows beyond the single recurring scenario driving the static $27/month consumption.
Build a phased Integration Suite activation roadmap against the $1,386 unconsumed budget, sequencing S/4HANA-to-third-party EDI flows, API-led B2B integrations, and event-driven supply chain connections to convert the platform from a static bridge into a governed integration backbone.
Solution Area
Customer Experience
22.3%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$2K
YTD Budget$9K
YTD ACV: $17K
Projected Consumed: $5K · Projected Budget: $20K · Projected ACV: $41K
Solution Insights and Actions
Cloud for Customer is the sole Customer Experience footprint at Cleveland-Cliffs, meaning the LoB has a unified sales and service front-office tool but lacks any surrounding CX architecture — no commerce, marketing, or CDP layer — leaving customer engagement confined to opportunity and case management rather than an end-to-end experience.
With no contract insights available for C4C, adoption cannot be validated against intent, which architecturally suggests the LoB may be operating C4C as a standalone CRM disconnected from ERP order-to-cash and service execution signals that would normally close the loop for a manufacturer like Cleveland-Cliffs.
Establish consumption and usage telemetry for Cloud for Customer to confirm whether sales and service modules are actively used or shelfware, since the absence of contract insight obscures whether the CX investment is delivering LoB value.
Validate the integration between Cloud for Customer and the core ERP (sales orders, pricing, customer master, service tickets) to ensure C4C is not operating as an isolated CRM island disconnected from steel order fulfilment and account economics.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
77.3%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$117K
YTD Budget$151K
YTD ACV: $194K
Projected Consumed: $280K · Projected Budget: $366K · Projected ACV: $466K
Solution Insights and Actions
IBP is architecturally positioned as Cleveland-Cliffs' unified planning backbone spanning demand sensing, inventory optimization, S&OP, and supply response — but the flat $19,419 monthly consumption signature indicates only a single planning workload is live, leaving demand, supply, and response planning modules provisioned yet dormant and the LoB without the integrated commercial-to-supply visibility the platform was contracted to deliver.
The isolated March spike to $38,839 against an otherwise 64% attainment pattern reveals planning activity is event-driven rather than embedded in a recurring S&OP cadence, meaning Cleveland-Cliffs is treating IBP as an episodic modeling tool instead of the continuous collaborative planning process it is designed to anchor — a gap that projects to an $86,840 FY2026 under-consumption.
Activate the dormant IBP demand and supply planning modules through a use-case expansion workshop to convert the flat $19,419 subscription baseline into multi-process adoption and close the $34,193 YTD budget gap.
Operationalize the March planning workflow in IBP as a standing monthly S&OP cycle with named planner ownership to convert one-time consumption spikes into a sustained cadence aligned to the FY2026 plan.
Cardinal Health Inc.
Life sciences and healthcare
YTD Consumed: $1.0M
YTD Budget: $1.4M
YTD ACV: $1.7M
Projected Consumed: $2.5M
Projected Budget: $3.5M
Projected ACV: $4.2M
EA Insights and Actions
Cardinal Health's three L1 domains each expose a different symptom of the same root cause — Commerce Cloud runs as a standalone storefront with no Service or CDC layer, Ariba's source-to-pay backbone is dormant while Digital Payments executes downstream, and SAP Build sits idle as a project tool — meaning the enterprise has bought the components of an integrated quote-to-cash and source-to-pay fabric but is operating them as disconnected islands, blocking the closed-loop digital operating model a healthcare distributor needs for margin control and supply assurance.
The BTP extensibility and AI layer (SAP Build plus Joule Studio at 44% May attainment) is the common platform dependency that should stitch Commerce, Concur, and Ariba together through custom workflows and AI-assisted automation, but its sporadic 68% YTD consumption means Cardinal Health has no scalable extension fabric — every cross-domain integration or AI use case will either be built in-core (creating upgrade debt) or outside SAP entirely (fragmenting the architecture).
The contract value signal is structurally inverted — Concur T&E lines are pinned at 120%+ of ACV ceilings with a ~$45K projected overrun while Ariba procurement shows zero consumption and Build is tracking a $227 shortfall — indicating entitlement is concentrated in employee-expense plumbing while the strategic spend-governance and extensibility investments that drive enterprise outcomes are underfunded in usage terms, undermining the ROI narrative of the broader SAP footprint.
Convene a cross-domain architecture review spanning Finance/Spend, CX, and BAIP to map the Ariba–Digital Payments–Commerce–Build integration chain and define an activation sequence that restores closed-loop source-to-pay and order-to-cash governance.
Establish a BTP and SAP Build Center of Excellence with a governed backlog drawn from Commerce extensions, Ariba activation workflows, and Concur support-deflection automations to convert idle low-code/AI entitlement into the connective tissue across all three L1 domains.
Run an enterprise entitlement true-up workshop to rebalance ACV from over-consumed Concur T&E lines toward Ariba activation and Build/Joule enablement, aligning contract shape with the integrated architecture Cardinal Health actually needs.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
68.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$242
YTD Budget$354
YTD ACV: $642
Projected Consumed: $580 · Projected Budget: $807 · Projected ACV: $2K
Solution Insights and Actions
SAP Build is architecturally positioned as the low-code extensibility layer for citizen developers and pro-code teams to extend SAP processes, but Cardinal Health's sporadic consumption pattern — 68% YTD attainment with only one month exceeding plan — indicates Build is being treated as a project tool rather than an embedded development platform, leaving the LoB without a scalable extension fabric for custom apps and workflow automation.
With Joule Studio listed under the Build footprint and May attainment collapsing to 44%, the AI-assisted development and generative extension capabilities that should accelerate app creation are effectively dormant, meaning Cardinal Health is paying for an intelligent low-code platform but operating it as a basic dev sandbox disconnected from the broader BTP automation and AI roadmap.
Stand up a SAP Build Center of Excellence at Cardinal Health with a governed intake process and a prioritized backlog of 3–5 process automation and app extension use cases to convert the projected $227 shortfall into recurring platform consumption.
Activate Joule Studio and SAP Build Apps developer enablement through hands-on bootcamps for a broader citizen developer cohort, closing the gap between licensed low-code/AI capability and the narrow project-based usage currently observed.
Solution Area
Customer Experience
8.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$29K
YTD Budget$360K
YTD ACV: $659K
Projected Consumed: $69K · Projected Budget: $982K · Projected ACV: $1.6M
Solution Insights and Actions
Customer Experience for Cardinal Health is anchored on a single commerce pillar — SAP Commerce Cloud is the only deployed product, meaning digital storefront, catalogue, pricing, and order capture are running without adjacent CX layers like Service Cloud, Sales Cloud, or CDC, leaving the architecture transactionally capable but experientially shallow.
With no contract insights available for Commerce Cloud, the LoB's flagship CX investment is operating as a black box — there is no visibility into license utilisation, storefront scale, or AI feature uptake, so it is impossible to confirm whether Cardinal Health is realising the 'unified commerce' and 'AI innovation' value the platform is architected to deliver.
Establish a consumption and adoption telemetry baseline for SAP Commerce Cloud covering active storefronts, order volume, and feature utilisation to close the contract insight gap and validate ROI.
Assess extending the CX footprint beyond Commerce Cloud with Service Cloud and Customer Data Cloud to convert the standalone storefront into an integrated customer experience capable of post-sale service and unified identity.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
97.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$1.0M
YTD Budget$1.0M
YTD ACV: $1.1M
Projected Consumed: $2.4M · Projected Budget: $2.5M · Projected ACV: $2.6M
Solution Insights and Actions
Cardinal Health's Travel & Expense stack is the operational center of gravity in Finance and Spend Management — ExpenseIt, Reporting Services, User Support Desk, Conn/Stat/Web/Extract Svcs, and Preferred Success Concur are all running 120%+ of plan with multiple months pinned at the ACV ceiling, indicating Concur is deeply embedded in daily employee workflows but the entire T&E entitlement envelope is structurally undersized for the LoB's real demand.
The Procurement pillar (Ariba Buying & Invoicing, Contracts, Sourcing, SLP) shows no consumption signal while Treasury's SAP Digital Payments is actively scaling — this means the source-to-pay backbone that should feed governed spend into payment execution is architecturally present but operationally dormant, leaving Digital Payments to process disbursements without upstream contract, sourcing, or supplier governance and undermining the intended closed-loop spend management design.
Consolidate a single true-up across Concur ExpenseIt, Reporting Services, User Support Desk, Conn/Stat/Web/Extract Svcs, and Preferred Success Concur to right-size the T&E entitlement envelope against the combined ~$45K projected overrun and pair it with a User Support Desk deflection program to bend the escalating ticket curve.
Open an architectural review of the Ariba suite (Buying & Invoicing, Contracts, Sourcing, SLP) to determine whether these procurement capabilities are deployed-but-unused, replaced by a third-party tool, or stalled in rollout, and define an activation path so that Digital Payments executes against contractually governed spend rather than ungoverned invoices.
Cargill, Incorporated
Agribusiness
YTD Consumed: $4.1M
YTD Budget: $4.1M
YTD ACV: $4.0M
Projected Consumed: $9.7M
Projected Budget: $9.8M
Projected ACV: $9.5M
EA Insights and Actions
Cargill is simultaneously over-consuming on every transactional/operational SAP workload (IBP at $4.26M projected vs $3.97M, Ariba Buying & Invoicing at $3.86M, Concur stack ~$160K over, Commerce Cloud $14K over, BDC Public 149.8%) while the governance and orchestration layers that should bound them (Ariba Sourcing 16%, Ariba Contracts 45% declining, SAP Build descending, BTP Technology Foundation envelope $0) sit cold — meaning the enterprise is scaling spend and volume without scaling the architectural controls that convert that spend into negotiated savings, planned demand, or governed extensions.
The BTP platform layer is the single common dependency across Data & AI (BDC + Database Cloud breaching), Integration Suite (106.7%) and Joule Studio (118%) burning hot, while the generic Technology Foundation, SAP Build, and the Concur-adjacent Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement all sit at or near $0 — Cargill is buying BTP capacity in the runtime services it already needs but has not activated the extensibility, low-code, and integration surface required to keep customizations off the S/4 core, guaranteeing technical debt accumulation against its agribusiness digitization roadmap.
Cargill's source-to-pay, plan-to-deliver, and quote-to-cash chains are each architecturally truncated — Ariba runs without upstream sourcing/contracts, IBP runs as the sole planning node with no governed process attribution, and Commerce Cloud runs without CX context or personalization — so the LoBs are transacting at enterprise scale on disconnected islands, blocking the integrated commodity-to-customer visibility a global agri-food operator depends on for margin protection.
Convene a cross-domain BTP activation review spanning Data & AI, Technology Foundation, Finance/Concur, and Customer Experience to consolidate the breaching Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreements, true-up the ~$200K combined BTP overrun, and sequence Integration Suite, Build, and Joule Studio against the dormant envelopes that should be extending Concur, Ariba, and Commerce Cloud.
Run a source-to-pay architecture remediation workshop with Cargill's procurement and finance architects to reactivate Ariba Sourcing and Contracts as the governing front-end to the $3.86M Buying & Invoicing flow, restoring the negotiated-savings loop the suite was contracted to enforce.
Establish an enterprise consumption-governance and true-up program covering IBP ($297K projected over), Concur (~$160K over), Commerce Cloud ($14K over), and BDC/Database Cloud ($38.5K over) that resizes ceilings against operational reality and instruments workload-level attribution before reactive throttling distorts planner, buyer, and storefront behavior.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
135.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$63K
YTD Budget$47K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $152K · Projected Budget: $114K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
Cargill's BTP footprint is concentrated in two Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement contracts — a large BDC Public envelope ($103K) and a smaller Database Cloud envelope ($10.5K) — and both are simultaneously breaching ceiling, signaling that the LoB's Data & AI platform foundation is scaling as an integrated workload rather than isolated experiments, with compute (BDC Public) and persistence (Database Cloud) growing in lockstep.
The BDC Public agreement is the dominant architectural risk at 149.8% May attainment and a projected 36% annual overrun ($37.5K), while the Database Cloud tier shows the same March–May acceleration pattern at smaller scale — the consistency across both contracts indicates this is genuine workload expansion in Cargill's Data & AI estate, not a sizing anomaly, and the contract envelope no longer reflects the LoB's operational reality.
Open a consolidated true-up on both Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement lines (LPR868 BDC Public and Database Cloud) to resize the contracted ceiling against the projected $38.5K combined overrun before throttling or overage billing disrupts active Data & AI workloads.
Run a joint workload review with Cargill's platform architects to map which BDC Public and Database Cloud services are driving the March–May acceleration, separate strategic Data & AI expansion from unoptimized consumption, and stand up BTP cost-governance controls — tagging, quota alerts, service-level monitoring — across both contracts.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
107.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$87K
YTD Budget$81K
YTD ACV: $6K
Projected Consumed: $209K · Projected Budget: $195K · Projected ACV: $15K
Solution Insights and Actions
The BTP foundation is architecturally bifurcated — Integration Suite and Joule Studio workloads on the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement are running hot (106.7% and 118% attainment respectively) while the general Technology Foundation envelope sits at $0 consumed, meaning Cargill is leaning on BTP for integration and AI extensibility but has not activated the broader platform services that would round out the foundation.
Low-code extensibility is regressing while platform consumption accelerates — SAP Build has declined 20% from January to May ($899 → $718) even as Integration Suite and Joule Studio burn through budget, indicating Cargill is integrating and automating at the platform layer but not building the business-facing apps and workflows that translate those integrations into LoB outcomes.
Initiate a true-forward on the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement covering both the Joule Studio (~$51,936 projected vs $42,335 ceiling) and Integration Suite (~$146,496 projected vs $140,122 ceiling) workloads to right-size the contract envelope against actual consumption before overage friction sets in.
Launch a joint SAP Build and Technology Foundation activation workshop to pair stalled citizen-developer app projects with the dormant $634 BTP services envelope, recovering the descending Build trajectory by anchoring new low-code use cases to live integration and AI workloads already running on the platform.
Solution Area
Customer Experience
106.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$128K
YTD Budget$121K
YTD ACV: $131K
Projected Consumed: $308K · Projected Budget: $294K · Projected ACV: $314K
Solution Insights and Actions
Cargill's Customer Experience footprint is architecturally narrow — only SAP Commerce Cloud is active, meaning the storefront is running without adjacent CX capabilities like Context-Driven Services, personalization, or B2B accelerators, leaving the LoB with transactional commerce but no orchestrated customer journey or insight layer on top of the $308K projected spend.
Commerce Cloud is delivering proven value but is approaching a capability ceiling — the 108%→103.6% consumption deceleration against budget alongside a $14,297 over-plan trajectory indicates the platform is sticky and load-bearing, yet the lack of complementary CX products means incremental volume gains will plateau without expanding the architectural surface area.
Initiate an expansion discovery on SAP Commerce Cloud to scope storefront extensions, B2B accelerators, or Context-Driven Services that convert the $14,297 over-budget consumption into a broader FY2027 CX commitment.
Validate the April–May Commerce Cloud dip ($26,127 → $25,042) with Cargill's digital commerce team to determine whether it reflects seasonal buyer behavior or an early signal of storefront saturation requiring architectural extension.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
90.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$2.0M
YTD Budget$2.2M
YTD ACV: $2.2M
Projected Consumed: $4.8M · Projected Budget: $5.3M · Projected ACV: $5.2M
Solution Insights and Actions
Cargill's source-to-pay architecture is operationally inverted — Ariba Buying and Invoicing is transacting at $3.86M projected while Ariba Contracts (45% attainment, declining) and Ariba Sourcing (16% attainment, flat ~$4K/month) are dormant, meaning the LoB is paying invoices and cutting POs without the upstream sourcing events or contract governance that should be feeding those transactions, eroding the negotiated savings the suite is designed to enforce.
The Travel and Expense stack is the inverse problem — every Concur and Oversight component (Analytics 131%, Company Bill Statement 132%, Expense 126%, ExpenseIt 132%, Oversight Detect 137%) is structurally over-consuming, indicating T&E is the most mature, embedded LoB capability in Cargill's Finance estate and the contracted ceilings no longer reflect the operational footprint, while the BTP extension layer (Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement at $0) sits cold and unable to support custom analytics, integrations, or compliance extensions the over-burning Concur footprint clearly demands.
Reconnect the Ariba suite as a single source-to-pay flow by driving a sourcing-to-contract-to-buying activation play that lifts Ariba Sourcing event volume and Ariba Contracts authoring so that the $3.86M flowing through Ariba Buying and Invoicing is governed by negotiated agreements rather than off-platform terms.
Execute a coordinated true-up across Concur Analytics, Company Bill Statement, Expense, ExpenseIt, and Oversight Detect to right-size ceilings against the combined ~$160K projected overrun, and pair it with a Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement activation workshop that sequences BTP workloads to extend the T&E stack with the integrations and compliance services its over-consumption demands.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
107.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$1.8M
YTD Budget$1.7M
YTD ACV: $1.7M
Projected Consumed: $4.3M · Projected Budget: $4.0M · Projected ACV: $4.0M
Solution Insights and Actions
IBP is the only active node in Cargill's Supply Chain Management planning architecture, meaning demand sensing, S&OP, inventory optimization, and supply response all converge on a single platform — making any consumption volatility on LPR318 a direct risk to the LoB's entire integrated planning capability, not just a budget line item.
The Q1 overburn pattern ($371,936/month vs $330,610 budget) followed by an abrupt return to exactly 100% in Apr–May signals reactive throttling rather than governed planning cadence, and with projected annual consumption of $4,264,862 already $297,548 above the $3,967,314 ceiling, the LoB is consuming IBP's AI-powered demand and multilevel supply planning capabilities without an architectural model for which planning processes justify the load.
Instrument IBP planning workloads by process type (demand sensing, S&OP cycles, supply response, scenario simulations) to attribute the $123,978 Q1 overage to specific capabilities and establish which are core recurring versus discretionary scenario runs.
Establish an IBP consumption governance framework that caps ad-hoc scenario and simulation runs against the remaining $2,314,288 envelope while protecting committed S&OP and demand planning cycles, preventing further reactive throttling that distorts planner behavior.
Conagra Brands, Inc.
Consumer products
YTD Consumed: $533K
YTD Budget: $548K
YTD ACV: $481K
Projected Consumed: $1.3M
Projected Budget: $1.5M
Projected ACV: $1.2M
EA Insights and Actions
Integration Suite is running at 175% of plan while BDC Public is 91% above plan, yet Database Cloud, Joule Studio, Technology Foundation Other, SAC BI, Spend Analysis, and the Finance BTP allocation all sit dormant — Conagra has effectively narrowed a multi-pillar SAP platform into an integration-and-pipeline utility, blocking the AI, analytics, and planning outcomes the contracted footprint was sized to deliver.
The analytical backbone is broken in parallel across domains — Spend Analysis at zero feeding a live Ariba sourcing stack, and SAC BI at 34% sitting on top of an aggressively growing BDC Public data layer — meaning Conagra is generating transactional and data volume faster than it can convert it into category, supplier, or enterprise decisions, undermining the margin-recovery and supply-resilience agenda central to a CPG operator in FY26.
BTP is contracted as the common fabric beneath Data & AI, Technology Foundation, and Finance, but with the Finance CPEA at zero, Joule Studio at 44%, and Technology Foundation Other fully dormant, there is no active extensibility or AI-agent layer connecting profitability, planning, and procurement — every cross-domain workflow (profitability-to-plan, spend-to-sourcing, data-to-decision) is left to point integrations rather than the governed clean-core architecture Conagra paid for.
Commission an enterprise EA rebalancing review across the BAIP Technology Foundation and Data & AI CPEAs to redirect the ~$21K of dormant Joule Studio, Technology Foundation Other, and Database Cloud capacity toward the Integration Suite and BDC Public overages before any true-up is priced.
Run a cross-domain analytics activation workshop spanning Finance (Spend Analysis, Profitability) and Data & AI (SAC BI, SAC Planning) to re-establish the closed-loop spend-to-sourcing and profitability-to-planning chains, recovering the combined ~$236K of dormant analytical contract value.
Launch a BTP clean-core and Joule governance review with the LoB architecture council to assign concrete extension, agent, and integration use cases to the dormant Finance CPEA, Joule Studio, and Technology Foundation Other buckets, preventing customisations from accreting in the S/4 core.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
162.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$110K
YTD Budget$68K
YTD ACV: $2K
Projected Consumed: $264K · Projected Budget: $234K · Projected ACV: $5K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI foundation shows a split-brain adoption pattern — BDC Public CPEA is consuming at 91% above plan ($106K vs $56K) while Database Cloud CPEA sits at 28% attainment, indicating Conagra is aggressively scaling application and AI workloads on the public tier while the underlying data persistence layer remains underutilized, a topology that risks compute-heavy pipelines running without a governed data backbone.
SAC BI is architecturally positioned as the decision layer on top of the Data & AI stack but its flat $100–150/month consumption and May drop to 34% attainment reveal it is operating as a static report viewer rather than the unified analytics-and-planning surface it is designed to be, meaning Conagra is investing in data platform expansion without translating it into broader business intelligence or planning outcomes.
Rebalance the two CPEA agreements by mapping the $43K projected BDC Public overage workloads against the $10.5K of dormant Database Cloud capacity to determine whether data services should be repositioned onto the under-consumed agreement before pursuing a true-up.
Activate SAC BI's planning and Joule-driven capabilities by sponsoring a use-case expansion into finance or supply chain planning domains, converting the dormant ~$2,100 of contracted BI capacity into the analytics-plus-planning outcome the product is architected to deliver.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
111.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$65K
YTD Budget$58K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $156K · Projected Budget: $143K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
The Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is functioning as a de facto Integration Suite vehicle — Integration Suite is consuming 175% of budget while Joule Studio sits at 44% and the Technology Foundation Other allocation is fully dormant, meaning Conagra's technology foundation has collapsed into a single integration workload rather than the diversified platform (AI, extensions, data services) the EA was contracted to enable.
The EA's architectural intent — a pooled platform credit to fund extensions, integration, and AI capabilities across the LoB — is being undermined by siloed consumption: Integration Suite is structurally underfunded against real demand while $4,936 of Technology Foundation Other and ~$16K of Joule Studio capacity sit unused, indicating the LoB has no governance layer reallocating EA credits to where workloads actually live.
Run a cross-bucket EA rebalancing review on LPR868 to redirect the dormant Technology Foundation Other allocation and the underused Joule Studio headroom toward the Integration Suite overage, and size a true-up only for the residual gap.
Launch a Joule Studio and Technology Foundation activation workshop to identify concrete extension, agent, and data service use cases that consume the unused EA capacity, breaking the pattern where Integration Suite is the only live workload on the platform.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
84.9%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$358K
YTD Budget$422K
YTD ACV: $479K
Projected Consumed: $860K · Projected Budget: $1.1M · Projected ACV: $1.1M
Solution Insights and Actions
The procurement architecture is fracturing at its analytical core — Ariba Sourcing, SLP, and Supplier Risk are all running at or below plan while Ariba Spend Analysis has collapsed to zero consumption since April, meaning Conagra is executing sourcing events and managing suppliers without the spend visibility layer that should be directing category strategy and supplier segmentation decisions.
The FP&A stack is operating in disconnected halves — SAC Planning is drifting downward in steady-state use while Profitability and Performance Management spiked late and Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement sits at zero, indicating profitability models are being built in isolation from the planning cycles and BTP integration fabric that should make them a continuous steering capability rather than a one-time modeling exercise.
Re-activate Ariba Spend Analysis as the upstream feeder to Ariba Sourcing, SLP, and Supplier Risk by anchoring it to a recurring category spend review cadence, recovering the $87,972 of projected unused contract value and restoring the analytical foundation the rest of the procurement suite depends on.
Convene a joint FP&A and BTP activation workshop to tie Profitability and Performance Management outputs into SAC Planning workstreams and operationalize the dormant Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement, converting the May profitability spike into a repeatable monthly cadence and closing the combined $148,742 consumption gap across the three products.
Coop Regions Of Organic Producer
Agribusiness
YTD Consumed: $119K
YTD Budget: $127K
YTD ACV: $135K
Projected Consumed: $286K
Projected Budget: $306K
Projected ACV: $325K
EA Insights and Actions
Across all four L1 domains, Coop Regions has provisioned the semantic, planning, and execution layers (Datasphere, SAC, IBP, SAC Planning) but left the connective tissue — Joule Studio at $0, SAP Build flat at $14, and no Ariba/Concur — dormant, meaning the enterprise is a federation of single-workload tools rather than the closed-loop planning-to-execution architecture the contracts describe.
Integration Suite is the only domain running at or above plan while every consuming domain (Data & AI at sub-70%, Finance at 74% YTD, Supply Chain anchored to a flat baseline) underperforms, indicating the integration backbone is moving messages for a narrow set of point integrations rather than enabling the cross-domain data flows that would lift Datasphere, SAC Planning, and IBP simultaneously.
The combined ~$220K ACV footprint shows a consistent pattern of baseline-only consumption — IBP at exactly 100% of plan, SAC BI plateaued at 34%, Datasphere trailing by $15,779, Joule Studio at zero — revealing an enterprise that is paying for a transformation-grade platform but operating it as a maintenance-grade utility, which will undermine renewal economics across every L1 area at once.
Convene a cross-domain architecture review spanning Data & AI, Finance, and Supply Chain to map the missing Datasphere→SAC Planning→IBP data contracts and establish Integration Suite flows that turn the three dormant planning capabilities into one closed-loop process.
Run a BTP activation workshop targeting Joule Studio and SAP Build, scoping a first agent/extension that consumes Integration Suite APIs to extend IBP or SAC Planning, converting the dormant innovation layer into a visible cross-domain capability.
