Launch a contract-rebalancing review across the portfolio to align the $15,779 Datasphere shortfall, $819 Integration Suite overage, $2,500 SAC Planning gap, and IBP baseline draw into a single consumption plan tied to the missing Finance and Supply Chain execution products.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
70.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$18K
YTD Budget$26K
YTD ACV: $33K
Projected Consumed: $44K · Projected Budget: $64K · Projected ACV: $80K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI stack is architecturally coherent but operationally truncated — Datasphere is provisioned as the unified semantic data layer feeding SAC BI, yet both products are running at sub-70% attainment with flat-to-declining curves, meaning the LoB has built the pipes and the visualization layer but is consuming them as a narrow point solution rather than the enterprise data fabric and decision platform they were contracted to be.
SAC BI's plateau at ~$197/month tracking to 34% of the $6,915 annual contract reveals that the planning, AI-driven decisioning, and Joule capabilities are dormant — Coop is using SAC as a static reporting viewer while the Datasphere Analytic Models underneath remain under-consumed, leaving forward-looking financial and operational planning entirely unaddressed despite the semantic foundation being in place.
Activate SAP Datasphere replication flows and expose additional Analytic Models to SAC BI for member cooperative performance, supply chain, and sales domains to convert the dormant semantic layer into consumable data products and close the $15,779 Datasphere projected shortfall.
Drive SAP Analytics Cloud BI beyond reporting by enabling planning scenarios and Joule-assisted analytics with author-level enablement for new personas, converting passive license holders into active planners and lifting consumption above the $572 monthly budget line.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
104.3%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$26K
YTD Budget$25K
YTD ACV: $21K
Projected Consumed: $62K · Projected Budget: $60K · Projected ACV: $50K
Solution Insights and Actions
The technology foundation is split between an active integration backbone and a dormant innovation layer — Integration Suite and its underlying BTP entitlement are running at or above plan while Joule Studio's Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement sits at $0 consumption, meaning Coop Regions is operating SAP integrations in production but has no AI/agent extensibility capability activated on top of them.
SAP Build is functioning as a fixed-utility tenant rather than a low-code innovation platform — the flat $14 monthly baseline with one isolated spike indicates a single recurring artifact is running, so the LoB has the app/workflow extension capability provisioned but is not using it to extend the core SAP processes Build is architecturally meant to amplify.
Activate the Joule Studio Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement by scoping a first agent or AI extension workload that consumes the integration APIs already in production on Integration Suite, converting the dormant entitlement into a connected capability rather than a standalone pilot.
Right-size the Integration Suite footprint by reviewing the May consumption drop and the BTP entitlement overage together — confirm whether declining message volumes free headroom to absorb the $819 projected overage on the SAP Integration Suite Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement before pursuing a contract uplift.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
95.3%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$18K
YTD Budget$19K
YTD ACV: $25K
Projected Consumed: $44K · Projected Budget: $46K · Projected ACV: $59K
Solution Insights and Actions
Finance and Spend Management for this LoB is architecturally thin — SAP Analytics Cloud Planning is the only active capability, meaning the customer has unified planning and analytics in place but no surrounding spend, procurement, or treasury products to feed operational data into the planning models, limiting the 'unify financial and operational planning' value the architecture is designed to deliver.
May's $4,249 consumption breakthrough on SAC Planning after four months of underperformance indicates the planning capability has moved from pilot to productive use, but YTD consumption of $18,157 against $24,628 ACV signals the LoB is using SAC Planning as a single-workload tool rather than the multi-domain planning platform it is architected to be.
Run a planning use-case expansion workshop on SAP Analytics Cloud Planning to convert the May consumption inflection into a sustained pattern and onboard a second workload — workforce, capex, or sales planning — to close the ~$2,500 ACV underconsumption gap.
Position adjacent Finance and Spend Management capabilities (such as SAP Ariba or SAP Concur) to feed actuals into SAP Analytics Cloud Planning, addressing the architectural gap where planning runs without integrated spend and procurement signals.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
100.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$56K
YTD Budget$56K
YTD ACV: $56K
Projected Consumed: $136K · Projected Budget: $136K · Projected ACV: $136K
Solution Insights and Actions
IBP is the sole Supply Chain Management product in the architecture at Coop Regions Of Organic Producer, meaning the LoB is running demand, supply, and S&OP planning in isolation without upstream execution or downstream response integration — a single-pillar planning stance that limits the closed-loop value IBP was designed to deliver for organic producer seasonality.
The perfectly linear 100%-of-budget consumption ($11,299/month, $56,495 YTD of $135,593) signals IBP is anchored to a recurring baseline workload rather than expanding into advanced modules like demand sensing or multi-tier supply planning, leaving the platform's AI-powered forecasting and collaborative S&OP capabilities under-leveraged relative to contracted capacity.
Audit active IBP modules against the $11,299 monthly run rate to confirm whether demand, supply, and S&OP planning are all in productive use or whether spend reflects a narrow baseline license draw masking dormant capability.
Activate AI-powered demand sensing and multilevel supply planning in IBP to address organic produce seasonality and convert flat consumption into measurable forecast accuracy and inventory outcomes that justify renewal.
Corteva Agriscience LLC
Agribusiness
YTD Consumed: $3.0M
YTD Budget: $3.0M
YTD ACV: $3.7M
Projected Consumed: $7.3M
Projected Budget: $7.2M
Projected ACV: $9.0M
EA Insights and Actions
Corteva has invested heavily in foundational runtime layers across the enterprise — Datasphere as the semantic core, Integration Suite at 3.5x capacity, IBP carrying all of supply chain planning, and Ariba Buying executing transactions — but the consuming intelligence and experience layers on top (SAC BI at 0.2%, SAP Build at 26%, Spend Analysis at $0, Catalog collapsed) are uniformly dormant, meaning Corteva is paying to move and store data and transactions without converting any of it into decisions, apps, or category intelligence, which structurally blocks the agriscience digital-thread outcomes (seed-to-shelf traceability, integrated demand-to-supply, category-driven sourcing) that justify the platform spend.
BTP is the shared substrate underneath Data & AI, Technology Foundation, and the integration fabric feeding Finance and Supply Chain, yet CPEA entitlements are fragmented across three LPR868 lines with one running at 207%, one at 359%, and one at $0 — this fragmented commercial posture means the platform that should enable clean-core extensibility, integration, and analytics is simultaneously over-consumed and stranded, exposing Corteva to overage penalties while leaving committed capacity unused and undermining the case for BTP as the single innovation platform.
Corteva's enterprise run-rate is projecting roughly $1.1M of overage concentrated in IBP ($987K) and BTP/Database Cloud (~$170K combined) against approximately $135K of stranded budget in SAC BI, Catalog, Spend Analysis, and Build, signaling that the contract portfolio was sized for a balanced architecture but consumption has bifurcated into 'runtime over, experience under' — without a portfolio-level true-up the LoB will hit hard ceilings on the platforms actually delivering value while continuing to pay for capabilities that are not landing.
Convene an enterprise architecture review across Data & AI, Technology Foundation, and Finance to activate the dormant consumption layers (SAC BI on Datasphere, SAP Build on Integration Suite, Spend Analysis and Catalog on Ariba Buying) as a single 'experience activation' program rather than four parallel domain fixes.
Initiate a consolidated BTP commercial true-up spanning all three CPEA (LPR868) lines plus the Integration Suite and Database Cloud envelopes to rebalance the over-run pools against the stranded $1,076 Technology Foundation and $1,939 BDC Public capacity before overage penalties materialize.
Launch a Supply Chain capacity and roadmap session for IBP to document the planning areas and cohorts driving the $987K projected overage and amend the LPR318 contract before consumption breaches the annual ceiling and stalls further planning expansion.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
28.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$12K
YTD Budget$43K
YTD ACV: $68K
Projected Consumed: $29K · Projected Budget: $105K · Projected ACV: $163K
Solution Insights and Actions
Corteva's Data & AI architecture is inverted — Datasphere, the semantic foundation meant to feed analytics and AI, is running a single static workload at ~24% projected attainment while SAP Analytics Cloud BI sits at 0.2% attainment, meaning the LoB has stood up a data layer with no consuming analytics experience on top of it and no business intelligence outcome being delivered.
The Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement split tells a bifurcated story — the Database Cloud footprint is running at 207% of plan ($12,819 projected vs $4,169 budget) while the BDC Public footprint shows five months of zero consumption against a $1,939 contract, indicating Corteva is investing heavily in database/runtime capacity but has never activated the platform services intended to host Datasphere and SAC BI workloads.
Activate SAP Analytics Cloud BI by standing up a first production story sourced from existing Datasphere models to convert the dormant analytics layer into a consuming application and unlock the $28,991 unrealized BI budget.
Expand Datasphere beyond its single static workload through a use-case onboarding workshop targeting two to three new business domains, and concurrently true-up the Database Cloud CPEA to right-size the $8,650 projected overrun against sanctioned data growth.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
184.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$187K
YTD Budget$102K
YTD ACV: $145K
Projected Consumed: $448K · Projected Budget: $284K · Projected ACV: $347K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Technology Foundation is architecturally bifurcated — Integration Suite and its underlying BTP entitlement are running at 3.5x contracted capacity ($230K projected vs $64K budget) while SAP Build sits at 26% attainment, meaning Corteva is heavily consuming integration runtime to connect systems but has not activated the low-code extension layer that would let the LoB build differentiated apps on top of those integrations.
A second Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement line tied to Technology Foundation shows $0 consumption against budget while the Integration Suite and Joule Studio CPEA lines are massively over-running — this signals fragmented BTP entitlement management where some capacity pools are exhausted and others are stranded, preventing the LoB from rebalancing committed spend across the same underlying platform.
Initiate a consolidated BTP commercial true-up across all three Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement (LPR868) lines to resize the $64K Integration Suite ceiling against the $230K run rate, reallocate the dormant $1,076 Technology Foundation envelope, and lock in committed-use pricing before overage penalties materialize.
Launch a joint SAP Build and Integration Suite use-case workshop to convert the heavily-consumed integration backbone into business-facing low-code apps and process automations, closing the $29,506 Build shortfall by extending existing integrated processes rather than treating Build as a standalone sandbox.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
75.9%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$1.3M
YTD Budget$1.7M
YTD ACV: $2.0M
Projected Consumed: $3.1M · Projected Budget: $4.1M · Projected ACV: $4.7M
Solution Insights and Actions
Corteva's procure-to-pay architecture is fundamentally broken at the intelligence layer — Ariba Buying and Invoicing runs at 80% with Ariba Sourcing at ~53%, but Spend Analysis is dormant ($0 since January) and Catalog has collapsed to a $50/month trickle, meaning the LoB is executing transactions without category visibility or guided buying, which structurally caps sourcing savings and drives the off-platform maverick spend visible in Ariba Buying's volatility.
The treasury architecture is contracted as an integrated cash-to-bank platform but is operating as four disconnected utilities — Cash & Treasury is declining (30% drop), Multi Bank Connect is locked to a static set of bank connections, Digital Payments is wired to a fixed transaction stream, and S/4HANA Customer Payments is capped at ~70% — collectively signaling that Corteva has deployed the treasury stack but never activated the end-to-end payment factory, leaving roughly $17K of in-year treasury value unrealized.
Convene a procurement architecture review that re-activates Ariba Spend Analysis as the category intelligence layer feeding Ariba Sourcing event design and Ariba Catalog enablement, closing the $86K Spend Analysis gap and the $48K Catalog gap by making spend visibility the trigger for sourcing and catalog onboarding decisions.
Launch an integrated treasury activation program that ties Multi Bank Connect bank onboarding to Cash & Treasury liquidity scenarios, Digital Payments method expansion, and S/4HANA Customer Payments scenario coverage, converting four parallel underutilizations into a single payment factory ramp against the combined ~$17K shortfall.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
127.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$1.6M
YTD Budget$1.2M
YTD ACV: $1.6M
Projected Consumed: $3.7M · Projected Budget: $2.8M · Projected ACV: $3.7M
Solution Insights and Actions
Corteva's Supply Chain Management architecture is anchored on a single planning pillar — IBP is the only active capability, carrying demand sensing, S&OP, inventory optimization, and supply response on its own, which means accelerating adoption (158.1% May attainment) reflects the LoB consolidating end-to-end planning workloads onto this platform rather than incremental usage growth.
The IBP footprint is architecturally healthy but commercially under-provisioned — YTD consumption of $1,557,213 against a $2,750,095 annual budget projects to $3,737,308, signaling that the planning platform's expanding role across Corteva's supply chain has outpaced the contracted capacity envelope and will constrain further scenario, cohort, or planning-area expansion if left unaddressed.
Engage Corteva's planning leads to map which IBP planning areas, scenarios, and user cohorts are driving the May surge so the $987,213 projected overage is tied to documented supply chain outcomes ahead of a true-up.
Initiate a commercial review of Integrated Business Planning (LPR318) to expand capacity or amend the contract before consumption breaches the $2,750,095 annual ceiling.
Domino's Pizza LLC
Consumer products
YTD Consumed: $803K
YTD Budget: $866K
YTD ACV: $958K
Projected Consumed: $1.9M
Projected Budget: $2.1M
Projected ACV: $2.3M
EA Insights and Actions
Domino's has contracted a full data-to-decision-to-execution spine — HANA Cloud underneath, Integration Suite moving data, IBP planning supply, and Business Network reaching suppliers — but every layer above the integration backbone is under-consuming (HANA Cloud stranded at ~$35K, Build at 52.7%, Business Network operationally inert at $55,968/month flat draw), meaning the enterprise architecture is wired for closed-loop, AI-assisted supply chain orchestration but is operating as disconnected point capabilities that cannot deliver the resiliency and demand-sensing outcomes a pizza supply chain depends on.
Integration Suite running hot at 97.6% while SAP Build, Datasphere/SAC, and Business Network collaboration are dormant tells us Domino's is investing in pipes without endpoints — data is moving between systems but is not landing in low-code apps, analytics surfaces, or supplier collaboration flows, which blocks the store-operations and franchisee-facing digital experiences that should be the visible ROI of the BTP investment.
The architectural pattern across domains is consistent — transactional and integration layers consume predictably (Concur T&E over-burning at 108–134%, Integration Suite at 97.6%, IBP planning on track) while every governance, extension, and collaboration layer erodes (Ariba S2C declining four months, Custom Domain at 49%, Build at 52.7%, Supply Chain Collaboration inert), signalling Domino's runs SAP as a system of record but has not operationalised it as a system of differentiation, capping the enterprise's ability to compete on speed, supplier resiliency, and franchisee experience.
Convene a cross-domain Business Data Cloud architecture review spanning Data & AI, Finance, and Supply Chain to activate Datasphere and SAC on the existing HANA Cloud footprint and surface Ariba, Concur, and IBP data products into a single analytics fabric, recovering the ~$35K HANA shortfall while making procurement and planning decisions explainable.
Launch a BTP extension and collaboration workshop pairing every active Integration Suite scenario with a SAP Build app, Custom Domain endpoint, or Business Network supplier flow, converting the $1,919 Integration Suite overage signal into pull-through demand that closes the combined $19,532 Build, $1,182 Custom Domain, and $119,982 Business Network shortfalls.
Run a Source-to-Pay-to-Plan integration coherence review across Ariba, IBP, and Business Network to reconnect declining sourcing and contract governance with active planning cycles and dormant supplier collaboration, restoring the closed loop from S&OP output to PO commitment that Domino's franchise supply chain requires.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
75.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$41K
YTD Budget$54K
YTD ACV: $67K
Projected Consumed: $98K · Projected Budget: $133K · Projected ACV: $161K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI foundation for Domino's is single-threaded on HANA Cloud with no evidence of the surrounding Business Data Cloud fabric (Datasphere, SAC, data products) being activated — the LoB has a database engine but not the analytics and agentic AI layer it was architected to anchor, which explains why consumption is contracting rather than compounding.
HANA Cloud consumption is trending toward ~$97.6K against a $132.7K ACV, leaving ~$35K of contracted capacity stranded — architecturally this signals workloads are being throttled or running outside SAP rather than expanding into vector, data lake, or AI use cases the platform is designed to absorb.
Activate the Business Data Cloud companion capabilities (SAP Datasphere and SAP Analytics Cloud) on top of HANA Cloud to convert the database investment into an end-to-end data fabric for Domino's analytics and AI roadmap.
Run a HANA Cloud workload and right-sizing review with Domino's data engineering team to recapture the ~$35K projected ACV shortfall by onboarding net-new vector engine, data lake, and AI workloads onto the contracted capacity.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
81.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$88K
YTD Budget$109K
YTD ACV: $131K
Projected Consumed: $212K · Projected Budget: $261K · Projected ACV: $314K
Solution Insights and Actions
The BTP foundation is architecturally split — Integration Suite is running hot at 97.6% YTD with a projected $1,919 overage while SAP Build is decelerating to 52.7% May attainment with 43% of contract value at risk, meaning Domino's is moving data between systems but not building the low-code apps and workflows that would turn those integrations into business outcomes.
The platform's branding and developer-extension layers are dormant while the integration backbone scales — Custom Domain service is contracting to 49% YTD attainment and Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement has declined three consecutive months to 76.1%, indicating Domino's is consuming BTP for back-end integration but has not extended it into customer-facing branded experiences or net-new platform workloads.
Pair the SAP Integration Suite capacity expansion conversation with a SAP Build co-investment plan that converts active integration scenarios into low-code apps and workflows, closing the $19,532 Build shortfall by attaching app delivery to every new interface onboarded.
Run a joint Custom Domain and Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement architecture review to identify SuccessFactors, BTP, and customer-facing apps still on default URLs and net-new BTP services that can be activated, reversing the combined decline and recovering the $1,182 and $196 projected shortfalls.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
98.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$347K
YTD Budget$353K
YTD ACV: $434K
Projected Consumed: $834K · Projected Budget: $852K · Projected ACV: $1.0M
Solution Insights and Actions
The Source-to-Contract spine is eroding in lockstep — Ariba Sourcing, Contracts, and Buying & Invoicing all show four-month declining attainment, meaning the upstream RFx and contract authoring engines that should feed governed buying are slowing together, leaving Domino's procurement architecture executing transactions without refreshed competitive sourcing or contractual guardrails.
Travel & Expense is the inverse pattern — Concur Expense, ExpenseIt, Analytics, and Preferred Success are all consistently over-burning budget (108–134% attainment) with a combined ~$10K projected annual overage, indicating T&E is the genuinely embedded, value-realizing LoB capability in Domino's Finance & Spend stack and is architecturally outgrowing its contracted envelope.
Right-size the Concur contract family (Expense, ExpenseIt, Analytics, Preferred Success) in a single true-up to absorb the combined ~$10K projected overage and prevent a year-end consumption block on the LoB's strongest-adopted capability set.
Re-engage Domino's category managers on Ariba Sourcing and Contracts with a joint RFx-to-contract enablement push to reverse the four-month decline, restore contract governance over the active Ariba Buying flows, and recover the combined ~$10K YTD procurement budget gap.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
93.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$326K
YTD Budget$349K
YTD ACV: $326K
Projected Consumed: $783K · Projected Budget: $903K · Projected ACV: $783K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Supply Chain Collaboration layer is architecturally provisioned but operationally inert — Bus Netw Supply Chain Collaboration is burning a flat $55,968/month subscription with no usage variance, meaning Domino's has the supplier-facing fabric in place but is not exchanging POs, forecasts, or inventory signals with trading partners, which strands the resiliency and visibility outcomes the Business Network is meant to deliver.
IBP and Business Network are contracted as a connected planning-to-execution loop, but only the planning side is consuming entitlement predictably ($46,540 YTD against a $111,692 plan) while the collaboration side shows no volumetric supplier activity — Domino's is generating demand and supply plans in IBP without closing the loop to suppliers, leaving S&OP decisions unvalidated against real supply commitments.
Launch a supplier onboarding and scenario activation program on Bus Netw Supply Chain Collaboration covering PO, forecast, and inventory collaboration to convert the fixed $55,968 monthly draw into volume-driven adoption and recover the projected $119,982 annual shortfall.
Run an IBP value-realization review tied to the remaining $65,156 of FY2026 budget, mapping active planner usage and S&OP cycle outputs into Business Network forecast and PO collaboration flows so planning decisions reach suppliers.
Eli Lilly & Company
Life sciences and healthcare
YTD Consumed: $7.3M
YTD Budget: $7.6M
YTD ACV: $8.4M
Projected Consumed: $17.5M
Projected Budget: $18.8M
Projected ACV: $20.1M
EA Insights and Actions
Lilly has contracted a full SAP enterprise stack — Ariba P2P, IBP, SuccessFactors, Commerce, Signavio, SAC, BDC — but every domain shows the same architectural signature: the transactional or advisory layer runs hot while the connective tissue (BDC Public integration services, Integration Suite expansion, Process Intelligence, SAC Planning, Batch Release Hub) sits dormant, meaning Lilly has bought an integrated enterprise platform and is operating it as a collection of point solutions.
The Integration Suite stall (LPR868 down 54%, LPR499 plateaued at 89%) is the single architectural fault line propagating across the portfolio — it is why Ariba's $1.5M procurement overrun cannot feed SAC Planning's 1.8% attainment, why IBP's single-cycle freeze cannot reach Batch Release Hub's zero-activity quality workflows, and why Joule Studio at 195% has no governed APIs to consume, blocking the closed-loop pharma value chain from clinical trial through manufacturing release to commercial fulfillment.
Across five L1 domains (CX, HCM, BTM, Data & AI, Finance) Preferred Success and advisory wrappers are consuming at 114–195% of ACV while the underlying platforms they accelerate sit at 1.8–44% attainment, meaning Lilly is paying premium rates for enablement of capabilities it has not operationalized — a pattern that signals strong transformation intent, weak landing discipline, and material renewal risk on the $250K+ ACV platforms those services are wrapped around.
Convene a cross-domain Integration Architecture Review anchored on Integration Suite and BDC Public to redirect the ~$255K LPR868 and $41.9K BDC Public unconsumed budget toward the specific API, event, and AI-agent integrations required to connect Ariba spend → SAC Planning, IBP → Batch Release Hub, and Joule → S/4HANA business processes.
Establish an enterprise-wide Preferred Success Realignment governance forum that converts the over-consumed advisory capacity across CX, HCM, BTM, Data & AI, and Finance into named platform activation tracks (Commerce modules, HXM lifecycle, Process Intelligence, SAC Planning, BDC AI services) tied to LoB process owners rather than consumed as standalone guidance.
Run a consolidated commercial true-up across Ariba (P2P), SAC BI, Joule Studio, and BTP Database Cloud — collectively $1.5M+ in overruns — paired with reallocation of the IBP, BDC Public, Commerce, and Compliance shortfalls into a single enterprise contract reshape rather than isolated product conversations.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
108.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$70K
YTD Budget$65K
YTD ACV: $14K
Projected Consumed: $168K · Projected Budget: $152K · Projected ACV: $34K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI foundation at Lilly is bifurcating: BTP on the Database Cloud tier is running 135.6% hot with $20,928 of projected overage, while the BDC Public tier collapsed 79% to $1,581/month — meaning data-tier workloads (HANA Cloud, persistence) are scaling with real business demand while the broader integration/extension/AI services on BDC Public are being abandoned or migrated, leaving the platform strategy lopsided toward storage and starved of the application and AI extensibility layer it was contracted to deliver.
SAC BI is the one capability proving its architectural value — ceiling-hugging consumption at exactly the $2,819 ACV cap in April and May after a 125% Q1 burn confirms Lilly's analysts are actively dependent on the dashboards, but with BDC Public integration services dormant SAC is operating as a standalone visualization tool rather than the unified analytics-and-planning fabric (Joule, planning, AI) it is designed to be, so Lilly sees the past clearly but cannot act forward.
Initiate a true-up on SAP Analytics Cloud BI to lift the $32,379 annual ceiling against the projected $6,500 overconsumption and prevent forced throttling of the only Data & AI capability with demonstrated business reliance.
Run a joint workload discovery across both Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement tiers to reconcile the $20,928 Database Cloud overage with the $11,838 BDC Public shortfall, then recommit the $41,929 of unconsumed BDC Public budget to named integration, AI, and planning extensions that connect SAC BI to the data workloads already running hot on the database tier.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
66.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$196K
YTD Budget$295K
YTD ACV: $151K
Projected Consumed: $472K · Projected Budget: $708K · Projected ACV: $362K
Solution Insights and Actions
The integration backbone is architecturally split and directionally inconsistent — SAP Integration Suite (LPR499) is plateaued on a fixed set of interfaces at ~89% attainment while the broader Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement (LPR868) Integration Suite footprint has fallen 54% since January, meaning Lilly is running legacy iFlows steady-state but has stopped expanding the API, event, and AI-agent integration surface that this foundation was sized to deliver.
The Joule Studio workload on LPR868 is the only capability showing real architectural pull — at 195% monthly attainment and tracking to $101.9K against a $59.2K budget, AI agent development is outpacing the contract envelope while the adjacent Integration Suite capacity that should expose secure APIs for those agents to consume is going unused, creating a foundation where agent build is racing ahead of the governed integration layer meant to support it.
Right-size the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement (LPR868) ceiling against the $101,900 projected Joule Studio run rate and reallocate the ~$255K of unconsumed LPR868 Integration Suite budget toward AI-consumable APIs that connect Joule agents to Lilly business processes.
Run an Integration Suite (LPR499) expansion workshop with Lilly's integration CoE to activate dormant API Management, Trading Partner Management, and Event Mesh capabilities against R&D and supply chain scenarios, lifting consumption above the $28.4K monthly budget line and closing the $38K projected annual under-consumption.
Solution Area
Business Transformation Management
48.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$47K
YTD Budget$96K
YTD ACV: $186K
Projected Consumed: $112K · Projected Budget: $245K · Projected ACV: $447K
Solution Insights and Actions
Eli Lilly's Business Transformation Management stack is architecturally split: Process Management (modeling/design) and Process Intelligence (mining/measurement) should form a closed-loop discover→model→improve cycle, but Process Intelligence went live months late and Process Management usage is project-bursty, so the LoB has the design layer without the data-driven feedback layer needed to govern transformation outcomes.
Signavio Preferred Success is the only product showing sustained, ceiling-bound consumption while the underlying Signavio platform products are underutilized — Lilly is consuming enablement and guidance services faster than they're operationalizing the tools those services are meant to accelerate, indicating strong transformation intent but weak capability landing.
Pair SAP Signavio Process Intelligence with SAP Signavio Process Management by onboarding Procure-to-Pay and Order-to-Cash into Process Intelligence and linking the mined models back to published Process Management repositories to close the discover-to-design loop and absorb the $130K Intelligence budget gap and $18.7K Management ACV gap.
Convert Signavio Preferred Success ceiling-bound engagement into platform adoption by redirecting the success plan toward activating idle Process Management modeler capacity and operationalizing Process Intelligence dashboards with named Lilly process owners.
Solution Area
Customer Experience
65.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$121K
YTD Budget$185K
YTD ACV: $311K
Projected Consumed: $290K · Projected Budget: $462K · Projected ACV: $747K
Solution Insights and Actions
Eli Lilly's Customer Experience architecture is inverted — the Preferred Success enablement wrapper is being consumed at 114% while the underlying SAP Commerce Cloud platform it is meant to accelerate sits at 44% attainment on a flat $12K/month workload, meaning the customer is buying guidance faster than they are operationalizing the platform that guidance is supposed to unlock.
Commerce Cloud is architecturally deployed but functionally narrow — the static consumption curve indicates only core storefront/catalog capabilities are live while personalization, promotions, and order management remain dormant, leaving Lilly without the unified omnichannel commerce experience the platform is contracted to deliver and putting ~$252K of ACV at renewal risk.
Redirect Preferred Success Commerce (LPR1042) engagements toward activating dormant SAP Commerce Cloud modules — personalization, promotions, and order management — so the over-consumed advisory capacity converts into platform adoption rather than depleting in isolation.
Run a Commerce Cloud (LPR823) storefront expansion workshop with Lilly to extend B2B/B2C catalogs and geographies, breaking the $12,049/month consumption ceiling and closing the gap against the $326,566 ACV ahead of renewal.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
99.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$5.8M
YTD Budget$5.8M
YTD ACV: $6.3M
Projected Consumed: $13.8M · Projected Budget: $14.4M · Projected ACV: $15.1M
Solution Insights and Actions
Eli Lilly's procure-to-pay backbone is architecturally complete and operationally dominant — Ariba Sourcing, Buying & Invoicing, and Preferred Success are all running at or above ACV ceilings, meaning the LoB is executing strategic sourcing through invoice automation at scale but is structurally under-contracted, with a combined projected overrun exceeding $1.5M across the three procurement products.
The finance analytics and compliance layer is dormant while procurement runs hot — SAC Planning sits at 1.8% attainment and Document & Reporting Compliance at 0.19%, meaning Lilly is generating high-volume procurement transactions through Ariba with no forward-looking financial planning consuming that spend data and no statutory e-document layer governing it, leaving ~$924K of planning capacity and ~$10.7K of compliance capacity stranded.
Right-size Ariba Buying and Invoicing (LPR746), Ariba Sourcing (LPR638), and Preferred Success Ariba (LPR837) through a consolidated procurement true-up that addresses the $677K, $447K shortfall-at-ceiling, and $175K overruns together rather than as isolated commercial conversations.
Launch a joint SAC Planning (LPR001) and Document & Reporting Compliance (LPR807) activation track that connects Ariba spend data into net-new OpEx/workforce planning models and wires S/4HANA document flows into the compliance engine, converting the dormant $935K+ finance capacity into active LoB capability.
Solution Area
Human Capital Management
98.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$343K
YTD Budget$349K
YTD ACV: $371K
Projected Consumed: $824K · Projected Budget: $846K · Projected ACV: $889K
Solution Insights and Actions
Lilly's HCM learning architecture is functionally bifurcated — SF Validated Learning is carrying the regulated GxP/clinical training load at ~$56K/month while standard SF Learning runs at ~$440/month, confirming the LMS is deployed primarily as a compliance system of record for life sciences validation rather than as a broad workforce development platform.
The SuccessFactors core is architecturally healthy but commercially mis-sized — HXM Operations is tracking to a 6.9% underrun ($694) while Preferred Success SFSF is consuming 118% of plan, meaning Lilly is leaning heavily on SAP's success services and expert guidance to operate a platform whose transactional footprint hasn't yet expanded to match, signaling unrealized HXM scope rather than over-served support.
Expand SF Validated Learning into additional regulated populations (GxP, clinical, manufacturing SOPs) and convert remaining classroom compliance curricula into validated items to recover the $43,546 projected annual shortfall and fully exploit the validated LMS as Lilly's compliance training backbone.
Run a joint Preferred Success SFSF and HXM Operations review to reallocate the $22,837 Preferred Success overage toward activating dormant HXM lifecycle events, manager self-service, and country rollouts that would close the $694 HXM gap and convert support consumption into platform adoption.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
87.8%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$742K
YTD Budget$846K
YTD ACV: $1.1M
Projected Consumed: $1.8M · Projected Budget: $2.0M · Projected ACV: $2.6M
Solution Insights and Actions
Eli Lilly's supply chain planning backbone is architecturally anchored in IBP but operationally frozen at a single recurring cycle — the identical $143,451 monthly signature shows only one planning process (likely baseline S&OP) is live while demand sensing, inventory optimization, and response & supply planning remain dormant, leaving the LoB with a partial planning fabric and a projected $209,372 ACV shortfall.
The GxP batch release capability is contracted but architecturally disconnected from Lilly's manufacturing operations — Batch Release Hub stalled after $144 of token consumption with zero activity in April and May, meaning quality decision workflows, electronic batch records, and release-by-exception dashboards exist on paper while actual batch disposition still runs outside SAP, defeating the compliance and lead-time reduction thesis of the investment.
Expand IBP beyond its single baseline cycle by activating inventory optimization and response & supply planning modules with Lilly's supply chain planning team, using April's $168,309 spike as the template for recurring multi-cycle consumption.
Stand up a Batch Release Hub activation pod with Lilly's quality and manufacturing leads to onboard the first production release-by-exception workflow at a nominated manufacturing site and convert the dormant $14,450 commitment into live GxP throughput.
H B Fuller Company
Chemicals
YTD Consumed: $449K
YTD Budget: $752K
YTD ACV: $951K
Projected Consumed: $1.1M
Projected Budget: $1.8M
Projected ACV: $2.3M
EA Insights and Actions
BTP is contracted as the foundational substrate across Data & AI and Technology Foundation — yet both Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreements sit at 0–9% YTD attainment with Integration Suite declining 14%, meaning H B Fuller has paid for an extensibility and integration fabric that does not exist in practice, forcing every Signavio, Ariba, Concur, and S/4HANA Private Cloud integration to either run point-to-point or live in the core — a posture incompatible with the clean-core, specialty-chemicals digital supply chain agenda.
The transformation flywheel is architecturally disconnected — Signavio Process Intelligence is mining processes, Signavio Process Management has tripled its ACV to model them, S/4HANA Private Cloud is the target system to realise them, and Ariba/Concur are the transactional sources that feed them, but with Preferred Success at 38% attainment, Ariba Contracts at $0, and BTP dormant, there is no governed pipeline from process discovery to ERP redesign, stranding the entire process-led S/4HANA transformation thesis.
The Procure-to-Pay spine is incomplete (Ariba Contracts dormant, Buying at 15%) while Travel & Expense is the only end-to-end operating chain (Concur Analytics, ExpenseIt, Extract at 110–123%) — demonstrating that H B Fuller can execute coherent multi-module architecture when ownership exists, but the absence of that ownership on the higher-value direct/indirect spend stack leaves negotiated savings, compliance, and S/4HANA finance close objectives architecturally unsupported.
Convene a cross-LoB BTP activation architecture review spanning Data & AI and Technology Foundation to bind the $10,520 Public CPEA, $10,883 Database Cloud CPEA, $5,841 unconsumed EA balance, and $18,190 Joule Studio gap to named Signavio, Ariba, and S/4HANA integration workloads before the combined ~$45K platform envelope becomes shelfware.
Stand up a Process-to-ERP architecture council linking Signavio Process Management/Intelligence outputs to S/4HANA Private Cloud Preferred Success entitlements (CoE, Expert Sessions, Technical Reviews) so the $78K Signavio ACV and $156K Preferred Success shortfall are reframed as a single governed transformation pipeline rather than two regressing line items.
Launch a Source-to-Pay architectural completeness review to activate Ariba Contracts against the live Ariba Buying and Invoicing flow and replicate the Concur T&E operating model — named owners, embedded analytics, contract-to-invoice data lineage — across direct and indirect spend.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
4.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$387
YTD Budget$8K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $928 · Projected Budget: $21K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
The Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is the foundational BTP runtime for Data & AI workloads across both Public and Database Cloud footprints, yet the Public tenant remains completely dormant at $0 consumed while the Database Cloud tenant only flickered to life in May with $387 — H B Fuller has contracted the platform that underpins extensions, integrations, and data services but has not stood up the workloads that would make those capabilities real.
With the Public CPEA at 0% YTD attainment against a $4,092 cumulative budget and the Database Cloud tenant at 9% YTD against $4,235, the Data & AI LoB is operating without an active BTP substrate, meaning any downstream analytics, AI, or data integration ambitions are architecturally stranded until a first workload lands on the platform.
Convene a Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement activation workshop targeting the Public tenant to identify and deploy a first concrete workload — extension, integration, or data service — that converts the $10,520 annual envelope from shelfware into live consumption.
Anchor the Database Cloud CPEA's isolated May usage into a repeatable production pattern by binding at least one persistent Data & AI workload to the tenant, protecting the $10,883 annual commitment from becoming a 10–15% utilization write-off.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
65.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$75K
YTD Budget$115K
YTD ACV: $107K
Projected Consumed: $179K · Projected Budget: $281K · Projected ACV: $257K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Technology Foundation is architecturally fragmented — Integration Suite is the only product with meaningful consumption yet it is declining 14% over five months, while BTP Enterprise Agreement capacity for Joule Studio, Integration Suite, and general workloads sits 44–100% unused, meaning H B Fuller has contracted a full platform foundation but is operating it as a single shrinking integration workload rather than an extensible architecture.
Custom Domain service is the only product trending on-plan at 104.6% YTD attainment, but with Integration Suite contracting and a third BTP EA bucket at $0 consumed, the LoB has paid for branded, secure endpoints in front of a platform that is increasingly dormant — the customer-facing wrapper is healthy while the underlying integration and extension fabric is hollowing out.
Launch an Integration Suite re-engagement review to diagnose the five-month consumption decline and onboard new iFlows or API-managed endpoints that restore monthly run rate above the $18K budget line and protect the $77K at-risk ACV.
Convene a BTP activation workshop against the fully-unconsumed $5,841 Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement balance and the $18,190 Joule Studio gap to commit 2–3 concrete extension, analytics, or integration workloads that move dormant platform capacity into active LoB use.
Solution Area
Business Transformation Management
60.9%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$227K
YTD Budget$372K
YTD ACV: $512K
Projected Consumed: $544K · Projected Budget: $894K · Projected ACV: $1.2M
Solution Insights and Actions
The Signavio transformation stack is architecturally bifurcated — Process Management (design/modeling) and Process Intelligence (execution mining) should operate as a closed loop where mined inefficiencies feed modeled to-be processes, but both products are simultaneously regressing in May, indicating H B Fuller has the tooling for continuous process improvement but no operating model connecting discovery to redesign.
Process Management's tripled ACV against collapsing consumption ($3,138 in May vs $8,845 in April) signals license procurement ahead of capability rollout — the LoB has bought seats for a broader process community without standing up the governance, ownership, or modeling discipline required to make Signavio the system of record for process documentation, leaving $78,129 of annual budget exposed.
Reconcile the SAP Signavio Process Intelligence -$49,543 May reversal with billing and delivery to confirm whether it is a credit, rollback, or genuine usage drop, then re-baseline the mining scope by onboarding additional source systems and process variants to close the $271,772 projected shortfall.
Operationalize the expanded SAP Signavio Process Management footprint by establishing a process modeling center of excellence with named process owners and a published O2C or P2P end-to-end use case, converting the dormant May ACV expansion into active modeling adoption tied to Process Intelligence findings.
Solution Area
Cloud ERP Private
38.3%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$40K
YTD Budget$106K
YTD ACV: $126K
Projected Consumed: $97K · Projected Budget: $253K · Projected ACV: $304K
Solution Insights and Actions
Preferred Success for S/4HANA Private Cloud is the sole adoption signal in this LoB's Cloud ERP Private footprint, meaning H B Fuller's success enablement scaffolding — CoE guidance, Expert Sessions, Continuous Quality Checks — is the only architectural lever currently active to de-risk their private cloud ERP transformation, and it is being underutilised at exactly the point where adoption coaching matters most.
The 50% month-over-month consumption drop and 38.3% YTD attainment indicate the success service is being treated as transactional rather than embedded in the S/4HANA roadmap — entitlements like Business Process Improvement and Technical Reviews are dormant, leaving the LoB to absorb private cloud operational risk without the architectural assurance the contract was designed to provide.
Convene a Preferred Success service review for S/4HANA Private Cloud to diagnose the March–April disengagement and re-map dormant entitlements (CoE, Expert Sessions, CQCs) to live S/4HANA workstreams before the $156K projected shortfall crystallises.
Activate high-value Preferred Success deliverables — Empowerment Sessions, Business Process Improvement, Technical Reviews — against H B Fuller's S/4HANA Private Cloud roadmap to close the $16,209 May gap and convert unused entitlement into measurable adoption outcomes.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
70.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$107K
YTD Budget$152K
YTD ACV: $205K
Projected Consumed: $257K · Projected Budget: $371K · Projected ACV: $492K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Procure-to-Pay architecture is fundamentally broken at H B Fuller — Ariba Contracts sits at $0 consumption while Ariba Buying runs flat at ~$980/month (15% attainment) and Ariba Buying and Invoicing shows volatile transactional spikes, meaning the LoB is processing invoices and purchases without the contract governance backbone that anchors negotiated savings and compliance.
The Travel and Expense stack is the only Finance and Spend Management capability operating as architected — Concur Analytics, ExpenseIt, and Connector/Extract Services all exceed budget (110–123% attainment) showing end-to-end embedded adoption, but the sudden 62% May collapse in Concur Expense breaks the chain and threatens the upstream capture-to-analytics data flow that the other Concur modules depend on.
Activate Ariba Contracts via a go-live readiness review and clause library enablement sprint to close the $11,190 dormant commitment and restore contract governance over the Ariba Buying and Invoicing transaction flow.
Diagnose the May Concur Expense drop by auditing submitter counts, approver queues, and configuration changes to restore the ~$12K/month run rate and protect the data feed powering Concur Analytics and Extract Services.
Hill-Rom
Life sciences and healthcare
YTD Consumed: $288K
YTD Budget: $378K
YTD ACV: $453K
Projected Consumed: $691K
Projected Budget: $877K
Projected ACV: $1.1M
EA Insights and Actions
Hill-Rom's Customer Experience and Source-to-Pay domains both exhibit the same architectural pathology — transactional layers (SAP Commerce Cloud at $37K, Ariba Buying at $65K) are running while the value-generating upstream and surrounding capabilities (personalization, Assisted Service, Ariba Sourcing at 23.4%, Contracts dormant at $53, Supplier Risk halving) are inert, meaning the enterprise is paying for end-to-end suites but operating them as isolated transaction engines that cannot deliver the closed-loop buy-side and sell-side intelligence the architecture was designed for.
Across both L1 domains the customer is over-consuming advisory and success wrappers (Preferred Success Commerce at 119–120%) while under-consuming the underlying platform capacity they are meant to unlock, indicating an enterprise architecture posture that is guidance-rich but activation-poor — Hill-Rom is buying the map but not driving the road, which directly blocks medical device commercial differentiation and procurement governance maturity simultaneously.
The combined ~$110K in annual projected shortfalls (Commerce $65,962 + Sourcing $21,216 + Contracts $15,983 + Supplier Risk $28,728 offset by Preferred Success over-run) signals a portfolio where contracted capacity exceeds operational integration capability — without an underlying integration and master data spine connecting suppliers, contracts, catalogs, and storefronts, every additional license dollar will continue to underperform regardless of solution-level remediation.
Commission an enterprise architecture review across Customer Experience and Source-to-Pay to map the integration spine (supplier master, contract repository, product catalog, pricing) and identify whether Integration Suite or BTP foundations are in place to carry the dormant capabilities back into the transactional flows.
Convene a joint Commerce and Procurement activation workshop with Hill-Rom's digital, sourcing, and legal leaders to convert the over-consumed Preferred Success guidance into a single prioritized activation backlog covering Commerce Cloud dormant modules, Ariba Sourcing-to-Contracts routing, and Supplier Risk embedding.
Initiate a portfolio-level capacity rebalancing review to right-size the Preferred Success Commerce uplift against the Sourcing, Contracts, and Supplier Risk shortfalls, converting unused procurement budget into funded integration and activation work rather than letting it lapse.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
Customer Experience
86.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$215K
YTD Budget$249K
YTD ACV: $284K
Projected Consumed: $516K · Projected Budget: $566K · Projected ACV: $683K
Solution Insights and Actions
Hill-Rom's Commerce architecture is bifurcated in its value realization — Preferred Success Commerce is over-consumed at 119–120% of budget while the underlying SAP Commerce Cloud platform runs capped at ~$37K against a $44.9K budget, indicating the customer is heavily leveraging success services and guidance but not expanding the storefront, B2B catalog, or personalization capabilities those services are meant to unlock.
SAP Commerce Cloud is deployed as a stable transactional storefront rather than the unified omnichannel platform it is architected to be — the flatlined $35.6K–$37K run rate and projected $65,962 annual shortfall show capabilities like Context-Driven Services, Assisted Service, promotions, and AI-driven personalization are dormant, leaving Hill-Rom's medical device commerce experience functionally narrower than its contracted footprint.
Initiate a contract expansion review for Preferred Success Commerce (LPR1042) to right-size the $63,026 annual capacity against the projected $78,919 consumption and convert the proven adoption momentum into a formal capacity uplift.
Launch a Commerce Cloud (LPR823) adoption sprint with Hill-Rom's digital team to activate an additional B2B storefront, customer segment, or dormant module (Context-Driven Services, Assisted Service, promotion engine) and close the $65,962 projected annual shortfall by extending coverage into untapped medical device order flows.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
56.7%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$73K
YTD Budget$128K
YTD ACV: $169K
Projected Consumed: $175K · Projected Budget: $311K · Projected ACV: $405K
Solution Insights and Actions
Hill-Rom's source-to-pay architecture is fundamentally broken at the strategic layer — Ariba Sourcing is in active decline (23.4% YTD attainment, dropping monthly) and Ariba Contracts is effectively dormant at $53 flatlined since January, meaning buy-side transactions on Ariba Buying ($65K consumed) are executing without upstream sourcing events or contract governance, which collapses the negotiated-savings-to-realized-savings chain the suite is designed to deliver.
Supplier Risk is architecturally disconnected from procurement decision-making — consumption frozen at $201/month then halving in May indicates external risk feeds are not embedded into sourcing or supplier lifecycle workflows, leaving Hill-Rom transacting $156K through Ariba Buying against suppliers whose financial, ESG, and geopolitical risk posture is not being evaluated at the point of award or PO issuance.
Reconnect the source-to-contract spine by launching a joint Ariba Sourcing and Ariba Contracts activation with procurement and legal to route live RFx events into the contract repository, closing the $21,216 sourcing gap and the $15,983 contracts gap with a shared pipeline of awarded-to-authored agreements.
Embed Ariba Supplier Risk into the guided buying and sourcing workflows already running on Ariba Buying and Invoicing so risk screening triggers on supplier selection and invoice approval, converting the $28,728 unused annual budget into mandatory risk checks tied to transactional volume.
Ingredion Incorporated
Consumer products
YTD Consumed: $441K
YTD Budget: $696K
YTD ACV: $602K
Projected Consumed: $1.1M
Projected Budget: $1.6M
Projected ACV: $1.4M
EA Insights and Actions
Ingredion has activated the semantic data layer (Datasphere at 102-112% attainment, ~$1,400/month run-rate) and the planning layer (SAC Planning live at 108% in May) but the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement underpinning both sits at $0 consumption across five months, meaning the AI-ready data and the planning models have no governed BTP integration fabric between them — blocking the closed-loop 'data-to-decision' architecture Ingredion's CPG/ingredients pricing and margin agility depends on.
The Source-to-Pay and FP&A domains are failing for the same structural reason — Ariba Spend Analysis at 2.4x ACV with Buying and Invoicing collapsing 45% leaves ~$480K of P2P value unrealized, while Profitability and Performance Management sits silent beneath an active SAC Planning tenant, meaning Ingredion can see spend and plan budgets but cannot enforce buying channels or attribute profitability, undermining the input-cost discipline central to an ingredients manufacturer's margin defense.
Across the entire SAP footprint only the T&E sub-stack (Concur Expense, ExpenseIt, Request, TripLink at 97%+ attainment) is architecturally complete and operationally embedded while every strategic layer — BTP foundation, Integration Suite, Joule Studio, PaPM, SAC BI — is either dormant or half-activated, meaning Ingredion is currently extracting value from a tactical traveler workflow while the transformational AI, integration, and profitability capabilities representing the majority of contracted enterprise value remain stranded.
Convene a cross-domain BTP activation architecture review spanning Data & AI, Finance, and Technology Foundation to bind the dormant Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement to named Integration Suite flows connecting Datasphere, SAC Planning, and Ariba, and to a first Joule Studio agent use case, converting stranded BTP credits into the integration backbone all three domains require.
Launch a Source-to-Pay-to-Profitability recovery workshop that uses Ariba Spend Analysis output to recapture the $480K Buying and Invoicing gap through catalog and PO-flip enforcement, and pipes the resulting governed spend into Profitability and Performance Management to feed SAC Planning with granular P&L dimensions.
Run a governance and audit-integrity review on Concur Intelligent Audit (78% YTD attainment) to close the policy-enforcement gap on un-routed expense reports and protect the only fully embedded end-to-end architectural chain Ingredion currently operates.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
105.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$50K
YTD Budget$47K
YTD ACV: $46K
Projected Consumed: $119K · Projected Budget: $107K · Projected ACV: $111K
Solution Insights and Actions
Datasphere is operating as the active semantic foundation of the BAIP stack — steady $1,300+ monthly burn and 102–112% attainment confirm it is embedded in recurring federated data workflows, making it the de facto unified data layer feeding downstream AI and analytics consumers at Ingredion.
The architecture shows a half-activated Data & AI stack — Datasphere's semantic layer is live and scaling, but SAC BI and the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement show no contract insights, meaning Ingredion is curating AI-ready data without a confirmed visualization and planning consumption tier to convert that data into business decisions.
Initiate a Datasphere capacity true-up with Ingredion to absorb the projected $1,243 annual overage and reprice the subscription against the demonstrated ~$1,400 monthly run-rate before unbudgeted consumption compounds.
Run a downstream activation review on SAC BI against the active Datasphere models to confirm whether business users are consuming the semantic layer for analytics and planning, and close the gap with enablement or expanded SAC entitlements where consumption is dormant.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
0.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$0
YTD Budget$1K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $0 · Projected Budget: $3K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
The Technology Foundation layer at Ingredion is architecturally contracted but operationally inert — the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement underpinning Joule Studio, SAP Integration Suite, and other BTP services has posted $0 consumption across all five reported months, meaning the foundational fabric for AI orchestration, integration, and extensibility is dormant and every dependent LoB capability is effectively un-enabled.
With Integration Suite unactivated, Ingredion has no governed iPaaS backbone connecting S/4 to surrounding systems, and with Joule Studio unused, there is no path to operationalize SAP's generative AI copilots — leaving the LoB unable to realize either the integration-led process automation or the AI-assisted user productivity that the BTP investment was sized to deliver.
Convene a BTP activation workshop anchored on the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement to nominate a first production integration flow on SAP Integration Suite and a first Joule Studio agent use case, converting the $1,012 of cumulative unconsumed budget into live workloads.
Establish a credit-burn governance plan that maps the remaining annual ceiling on the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement to named Integration Suite, Joule Studio, and Technology Foundation workloads, with executive escalation triggered on any further month of $0 consumption.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
60.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$391K
YTD Budget$647K
YTD ACV: $556K
Projected Consumed: $939K · Projected Budget: $1.5M · Projected ACV: $1.3M
Solution Insights and Actions
The Travel & Expense stack is the architectural success story for Ingredion — Concur Expense, ExpenseIt, Request, TripLink, Direct Travel, Reporting, and Preferred Success are all running at 97%+ attainment with April/May exceeding budget, meaning the full T&E capture-to-audit-to-reimburse process is operationally embedded and the LoB is realizing the intended end-to-end traveler and finance workflow.
The Source-to-Pay architecture is structurally imbalanced — Ariba Spend Analysis is consuming at 2.4x its ACV ($181K projected vs $75K budget) while Ariba Buying and Invoicing has collapsed 45% from March to May and is tracking to leave ~$480K of contracted P2P value unrealized, meaning Ingredion is diagnosing spend they cannot then control through governed buying channels and category leakage is the most probable explanation.
The FP&A platform layer is fundamentally broken — SAC Planning has finally activated in May at 108% but sits on a $23.7K YTD hole, while Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is at $0 consumption and Profitability and Performance Management is silent, meaning Ingredion has planning models with no underlying BTP extension fabric and no profitability engine to feed them granular P&L dimensions.
Concur Intelligent Audit is the weakest link in an otherwise healthy T&E chain at 78% YTD attainment with volatile month-to-month coverage, indicating expense reports are flowing through Concur Expense but only a sampled subset is being policy-enforced, leaving Ingredion with audit exposure on the un-routed remainder.
Launch a joint Ariba Spend Analysis-to-Ariba Buying recovery program that uses the over-consumed spend visibility to identify the off-platform categories driving the $480K Buying and Invoicing gap, then channel that demand back through catalogs and PO-flip workflows.
Convene a BTP and FP&A activation workshop that ties the dormant $134 Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement and unused Profitability and Performance Management capacity to the active SAC Planning tenant, establishing the integration and profitability-model backbone needed to scale May's planning momentum beyond a single team.

YTD Consumed$188
YTD Budget$4K
YTD ACV: $5K
Projected Consumed: $450 · Projected Budget: $9K · Projected ACV: $12K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI architecture at Kraft Heinz is inverted — the foundational BTP consumption layer (Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement, Public edition) is burning at 390% attainment while the analytics consumption layer (SAC BI) sits at 0%, meaning the LoB is paying for platform expansion that produces no decision-making output.
SAC BI is contractually live but architecturally dormant against an $8,651 commitment, so Kraft Heinz has no operational path to the catalog's intended unified analytics, planning, or Joule-driven AI capabilities — the BI layer of the Data & AI stack effectively does not exist for this LoB.
Launch an SAC BI activation plan anchored to one finance or supply chain dashboard use case to convert the $4,121 elapsed budget gap into first productive logins and unlock the unified analytics and planning capability the LoB has already paid for.
Open a Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement governance and true-up review to rightsize the Public-edition $145 projected overage while simultaneously activating the dormant Database Cloud $100 contract through a workload onboarding workshop, bringing both BTP entitlements into a single managed consumption plan.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
104.8%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$118K
YTD Budget$112K
YTD ACV: $27K
Projected Consumed: $283K · Projected Budget: $273K · Projected ACV: $64K
Solution Insights and Actions
The integration backbone is bifurcating — SAP Integration Suite (LPR499) consumption is decaying for three straight months ($5,030 → $4,328) while BTP-hosted Integration Suite workloads on the Enterprise Agreement are also softening (80% May attainment), indicating Kraft Heinz's API and iFlow estate is contracting on both commercial vehicles rather than migrating between them, which erodes the AI-ready integration layer meant to govern agent, app, and data connectivity.
Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement consumption is structurally under-contracted across every workload category — Joule Studio is running at 190% of plan, Technology Foundation services swung from 44% to 136% attainment, and total BTP burn is outpacing budget — signaling that production-grade AI and platform workloads have landed without a commercial envelope sized to support them, putting the entire Technology Foundation at risk of mid-year true-up before expansion conversations can occur.
Consolidate a single Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement (LPR868) workload review across Joule Studio, Integration Suite, and Technology Foundation services to attribute the cumulative overrun to specific BTP services and resize the $710 and broader annual budgets to the realized run rate before contract headroom is exhausted.
Launch an Integration Suite (LPR499) adoption sprint targeting net-new API management, event mesh, and B2B scenarios to reverse the three-month consumption decline, close the $977 May ACV gap, and re-establish the integration layer as the governed foundation for AI agent and application connectivity.
Solution Area
Business Transformation Management
55.8%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$172K
YTD Budget$308K
YTD ACV: $425K
Projected Consumed: $413K · Projected Budget: $750K · Projected ACV: $1.0M
Solution Insights and Actions
The Signavio process transformation stack is architecturally split-brained at Kraft Heinz — Process Intelligence (mining/discovery) showed real Q1 traction at 101.7% attainment while Process Management (modeling/governance) is stalled at 48%, meaning the LoB is discovering process inefficiencies faster than it can codify and govern the redesigned to-be processes, breaking the closed-loop transformation cycle Signavio is designed to deliver.
The -$71,055 May reversal on Process Intelligence is not just a finance anomaly but an adoption risk signal — it obscures whether mining use cases are genuinely embedded or were one-off discovery exercises, and combined with the sawtooth April-May pattern on Process Management, suggests Business Transformation Management is running Signavio as project-based tooling rather than as the continuous process intelligence backbone required to protect the combined ~$750K annual investment.
Reconcile the -$71,055 May Process Intelligence entry with finance and delivery to restate YTD position, then lock in Q1 mining momentum by committing Kraft Heinz process owners to named, recurring use cases against the $681,686 annual envelope.
Stand up a Signavio Center of Excellence that bridges Process Intelligence findings into Process Management governed models, converting April's modeling burst users into recurring contributors to close the $29,093 projected Process Management shortfall and restore the discover-to-govern loop.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
87.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$2.0M
YTD Budget$2.3M
YTD ACV: $2.7M
Projected Consumed: $4.8M · Projected Budget: $5.4M · Projected ACV: $6.5M
Solution Insights and Actions
The Source-to-Pay backbone is architecturally fractured at Kraft Heinz — Ariba Buying and Invoicing is running 11% over budget as the operational engine of daily procurement, yet Ariba Sourcing is flatlined at ~33% attainment, Ariba Spend Analysis has decayed to zero, and Ariba Supplier Risk has never activated — meaning the LoB is transacting at scale without the upstream sourcing discipline, spend visibility, or supplier risk governance that justify the Ariba investment.
The financial transformation stack is half-wired — S/4HANA Finance Cloud shows volatile, catch-up consumption (80.5% YTD with a February blackout) while Profitability and Performance Management activated only in April–May with a single lumpy workload, indicating Kraft Heinz has the harmonized ledger and profitability modeling capability contracted but is not yet running continuous close or recurring profitability analytics on it.
Stand up Ariba Supplier Risk and re-activate Ariba Spend Analysis as governance prerequisites to the Ariba Buying overrun, embedding risk-monitored suppliers and spend visibility into the same categories driving the $346K projected invoice overage before initiating the Buying and Invoicing true-up.
Convert the May Profitability and Performance Management spike into recurring monthly cost allocation and P&L cycles wired to S/4HANA Finance Cloud's Universal Journal, closing both the $1.19M PaPM consumption gap and the $25.8K S/4HANA Finance gap by making continuous close and profitability reporting a single operational rhythm.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
55.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$457K
YTD Budget$830K
YTD ACV: $1.3M
Projected Consumed: $1.1M · Projected Budget: $2.2M · Projected ACV: $3.2M
Solution Insights and Actions
Supply chain collaboration is architecturally live but operationally narrow — Bus Netw Supply Chain Collaboration is the sole active capability in Kraft Heinz's SCM landscape, meaning trading partner connectivity exists but the broader resiliency and forecast-collaboration value depends entirely on this single network spine without upstream IBP planning signals or downstream S/4HANA execution visibility being evidenced.
The volatile March-April-May consumption pattern ($75K → $143K → $125K) against 20.8% YTD attainment on a $2.19M budget indicates the network is being used for episodic supplier onboarding rather than steady document exchange, leaving procurement and forecast collaboration capabilities provisioned but not embedded in day-to-day buyer-supplier transactions.
Convert Bus Netw Supply Chain Collaboration from onboarding-driven spikes into a recurring PO and forecast collaboration baseline by mandating network-based document exchange for all tier-1 Kraft Heinz suppliers already provisioned.
Close the $860K projected shortfall on LPR985 by sequencing the next supplier onboarding wave against high-volume trading partners and activating forecast collaboration scenarios to lift transactional consumption above the $182K monthly budget threshold.
Lancaster Colony Corporation
Consumer products
YTD Consumed: $52K
YTD Budget: $85K
YTD ACV: $130K
Projected Consumed: $124K
Projected Budget: $208K
Projected ACV: $311K
EA Insights and Actions
Across all four L1 domains — Data & AI, Technology Foundation, Business Transformation, and Finance — the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is the common substrate, and its uniform zero-consumption state (combined ~$5,370 annual commitment, ~$700+ already unconsumed YTD) means Lancaster Colony has architecturally committed to a BTP-anchored extensibility model while operationally running a coreonly SAP estate, blocking every downstream cross-domain story from data products to AI-assisted FP&A.
The Signavio stack reveals a process-aware but transformation-incapable posture — Process Intelligence has traction while Process Management sits at 20.9% attainment — meaning Lancaster Colony can observe O2C/P2P execution but has no governed to-be model nor an Integration Suite fabric to operationalize improvements, leaving the CPG-critical objectives of margin protection, demand responsiveness, and S/4 clean-core readiness without an executable transformation pipeline.
The dormant Integration Suite entitlement is the single architectural chokepoint compounding gaps in every other domain — Finance cannot land planning data flows, Data & AI cannot establish a data fabric, Joule Studio cannot ground AI agents in business context, and Signavio process insights cannot trigger automated remediation — converting four independent shelfware patterns into one systemic integration deficit that will force every future initiative into point-to-point or in-core workarounds.
Convene an enterprise-level CPEA activation review spanning Data & AI, Technology Foundation, and Finance sponsors to designate Integration Suite as the first-activated service with a named cross-domain use case (e.g., FP&A planning data flow into Datasphere) that unlocks the $5,370 combined annual entitlement.
Commission a clean-core extensibility architecture review to inventory in-core customizations being built in the absence of BTP activation and redirect them onto the contracted Integration Suite and Joule Studio entitlements before S/4 upgrade debt compounds.
Launch a Signavio Process Management adoption sprint anchored on one end-to-end CPG process (Order-to-Cash or Procure-to-Pay) and bind it to an Integration Suite-enabled execution path, closing the $112.8K Process Intelligence gap and converting process visibility into governed transformation.

BAIP - Data & AI

Projected Consumed: $0 · Projected Budget: $2K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI foundation for Lancaster Colony is architecturally contracted but operationally inert — both the Public BDC and Database Cloud allocations of the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement show zero activation, meaning the LoB has no live BTP runtime, no data fabric, and no AI service surface despite committing to the platform.
With a combined $1,880 annual commitment and $712 already unconsumed through May, the entire Data & AI capability stack — integration, data engineering, and AI/ML services that downstream analytics and automation depend on — is sitting as shelfware, leaving the LoB without the substrate any future SAP data or AI initiative would need to land on.
Convene a Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement activation workshop covering both the Public BDC and Database Cloud entitlements to identify a first production workload — such as a HANA Cloud-backed data product or a Build/Integration Suite use case — and break the dual flatline.
Escalate the zero-consumption pattern on LPR868 to the executive sponsor with a prioritized BTP service ramp plan that converts the $712 cumulative gap into committed use cases and protects the remaining annual value from becoming total shelfware.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation

YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$0
YTD Budget$1K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $0 · Projected Budget: $4K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
The Technology Foundation layer is architecturally provisioned but operationally inert — Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement shows zero consumption across Joule Studio, Integration Suite, and broader BTP services, meaning Lancaster Colony has no active extensibility, integration, or AI foundation underpinning its SAP landscape despite holding contractual entitlement.
The absence of Integration Suite consumption is the most consequential gap, as it leaves the LoB without the iPaaS backbone required to connect SAP and non-SAP systems, while the dormant Joule Studio entitlement means AI/agent extensibility cannot be built on top of a non-existent integration fabric — a compounding architectural debt against the combined $3,304 annual commitment.
Activate SAP Integration Suite under the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement by identifying the first business-critical integration scenario and onboarding it to the tenant to establish the integration foundation that Joule Studio and other BTP services depend on.
Sequence a Joule Studio first-use case on Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement only after Integration Suite is live, ensuring AI extensions are grounded in connected business data rather than provisioned in isolation against the $1,966 entitlement.
Solution Area
Business Transformation Management
62.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$52K
YTD Budget$83K
YTD ACV: $130K
Projected Consumed: $124K · Projected Budget: $202K · Projected ACV: $311K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Business Transformation Management stack is architecturally bifurcated — Process Intelligence (mining/analyzing execution data) is the only capability with meaningful traction, while Process Management (the modeling, governance, and target-state design layer) sits at 20.9% attainment, meaning Lancaster Colony can see how processes run but has no governed to-be model to transform them against.
The May reversal in Process Intelligence (-28.9%, -$3,883) combined with Process Management's stop-start April-to-May collapse ($1,793 → $529) indicates the transformation toolchain is being used as a point-in-time analysis utility rather than an embedded continuous improvement loop, leaving $112.8K and ~$33K of remaining budget exposed across the two products.
Reconcile the SAP Signavio Process Intelligence May -$3,883 negative entry with billing operations to re-baseline consumption, then launch an expansion play binding a second mining workstream to a Process Management modeled process to close the $112,811 gap.
Stand up a Signavio Process Management adoption sprint anchored on a named end-to-end process (Order-to-Cash or Procure-to-Pay) with enabled process owners and modelers to convert April's usage spike into sustained monthly activity against the $3.4K–$3.6K budget plan.

Solution Insights and Actions
The Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is the foundational consumption vehicle for extending Finance and Spend Management capabilities — extensions, integrations, and custom analytics for FP&A workloads — and its zero-adoption state means Lancaster Colony's finance function has no active platform runway to build differentiating capabilities on top of its SAP core, leaving planned innovation architecturally stranded.
With $0 consumed against $71 of YTD budget on a $186 annual contract, CPEA is not a slow-ramp pattern but a dormant entitlement, signalling that no BTP services have been provisioned to support FP&A use cases like planning integrations, data pipelines, or finance-side extensions — the contract is funding optionality the LoB is not exercising.
Run a CPEA activation workshop with Lancaster Colony's FP&A team to identify the first production workload — likely an Integration Suite or Datasphere service supporting financial planning data flows — and convert dormant entitlement into an initial service draw-down.
Build a phased CPEA consumption roadmap sequencing 2–3 priority BTP services against named FP&A use cases to recover the $115 remaining budget and prevent forfeiture of the annual contract value.
