YTD Consumed$42K
YTD Budget$146K
YTD ACV: $236K
Projected Consumed: $100K · Projected Budget: $352K · Projected ACV: $567K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI foundation is architecturally inverted — Datasphere is running a single static workload at 6% attainment while SAC BI consumption is flatlining and declining, meaning the semantic data layer that should be feeding expanding analytics is instead constraining it, and the LoB is operating with a narrow reporting footprint rather than the unified analytics-and-planning fabric the contract envisions.
BTP is entirely dormant across both Public and Database Cloud agreements with $0 consumed against ~$3,741 cumulative budget, meaning the platform layer meant to host extensions, integrations, and AI services for Datasphere and SAC is non-existent — Data & AI is consuming SaaS analytics without the underlying platform plumbing, which caps any future AI-ready data or Joule-driven use cases.
Launch a Datasphere use-case expansion workshop to onboard 2–3 new source systems and consumer personas (planning, data science, AI) so the semantic layer feeds beyond the single static workload and unlocks the $220K of unrealized contract value.
Trigger a BTP activation workshop for Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement (both Public and Database Cloud) to provision a first production workload — Integration Suite or data services feeding Datasphere — and convert the $0 consumption pattern into a measurable platform footprint.

BAIP - Technology Foundation

YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$74
YTD Budget$8K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $178 · Projected Budget: $20K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
The Technology Foundation is architecturally contracted but operationally inert across all three pillars — Joule Studio (extensibility/AI), Integration Suite (process integration), and broader BTP services are all running at near-zero against a combined ~$13.5K annual entitlement, meaning Land O'Lakes has no active platform layer to build extensions, orchestrate integrations, or host custom workloads on top of its SAP estate.
The Integration Suite flatline is the most consequential gap — with $0 consumed against $6,833 annual budget, Land O'Lakes has no SAP-managed integration backbone in operation, forcing any cross-system process flows to rely on legacy middleware or point-to-point connections that will undermine downstream SAP investments requiring clean API and event-driven integration.
Run a single consolidated BTP activation workshop covering Joule Studio, Integration Suite, and core Technology Foundation services on LPR868 to identify the first production workload per pillar — starting with an Integration Suite iflow tied to an existing SAP-to-SAP or SAP-to-third-party process — and convert design intent into deployed consumption.
Assign a named platform owner at Land O'Lakes accountable for the LPR868 Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement with a workload onboarding backlog covering extension apps (Joule Studio), integration flows (Integration Suite), and data/runtime services, so the $13.5K annual entitlement is tied to specific business capabilities rather than left as unallocated capacity.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
100.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$521K
YTD Budget$520K
YTD ACV: $600K
Projected Consumed: $1.2M · Projected Budget: $1.2M · Projected ACV: $1.4M
Solution Insights and Actions
Land O'Lakes' Finance and Spend Management architecture is bifurcated: the transactional Concur T&E stack (Expense, ExpenseIt, Intelligent Audit, Reporting, Connectors) and Ariba Buying and Invoicing are all running 108–122% over plan, proving the operational backbone is embedded — but the strategic upstream layer (Ariba Sourcing at 60% and Fieldglass Services Procurement at 2.9%) is dormant, meaning the LoB is paying invoices and reimbursing expenses at scale without the sourcing events or SOW governance that would control what gets bought in the first place.
The procurement value chain is architecturally incomplete — Ariba Spend Analysis is running flat at sub-91% with no growth in spend domains, while Buying and Invoicing overshoots and Sourcing underperforms, so Land O'Lakes has visibility into transactions but no analytical feedback loop converting that spend data into new sourcing events or supplier consolidation, which is exactly the closed-loop outcome the Ariba suite is designed to deliver.
Reactivate Fieldglass Services Procurement by onboarding a defined set of high-volume SOWs and contingent labor suppliers to restore SOW governance over the external services spend that Ariba Buying and Invoicing is currently processing without upstream controls.
Expand Ariba Sourcing event coverage and Ariba Spend Analysis category scope in tandem so spend insights feed a sourcing event pipeline, closing the combined ~$30K of unused procurement entitlement and rebalancing consumption away from the over-burning downstream P2P products.

Solution Insights and Actions
The Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is delivering value through Database Cloud workloads at 16x the budgeted envelope while the Public BDC entitlement on the same contract sits completely dormant — Marathon Petroleum is consuming data persistence capability aggressively but has not activated the application extension, integration, or data service layer that BDC Public is architected to provide, leaving the data tier without a modern extensibility and integration fabric on top.
The contract is simultaneously under-provisioned and under-utilized across its two pillars, with Database Cloud projecting ~$118K against a $9,158 ceiling while BDC Public projects $0 against $3,300 — this split signals a one-dimensional Data & AI adoption pattern where the LoB is scaling raw database consumption without the platform services needed to turn that data into AI, analytics extensions, or governed business processes.
Initiate a true-up on Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement (LPR868) Database Cloud to realign the commercial envelope with the ~$118K projected run rate and review which services are driving the May spike to confirm production intent versus unoptimized configuration.
Run a BDC Public activation workshop on Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement (LPR868) to land a first concrete extension, integration, or data service workload that connects the heavily consumed Database Cloud layer to the dormant platform capabilities before the $1,621 remaining budget is forfeited.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
167.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$179K
YTD Budget$107K
YTD ACV: $110K
Projected Consumed: $429K · Projected Budget: $264K · Projected ACV: $264K
Solution Insights and Actions
The integration backbone is architecturally live and load-bearing — SAP Integration Suite is running at 95–109% of plan with a mid-year ACV uplift, confirming Marathon has standardized on it for active iFlows, but the post-uplift consumption deceleration indicates the newly purchased capacity for expanded integration scenarios has not yet been activated in build or production.
The Joule Studio footprint on the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is consuming at ~7x the budgeted rate while the same agreement's Integration Suite and Technology Foundation allocations sit dormant or near-zero — BTP is being used as an ungoverned AI/agent sandbox rather than as the unified extension and integration foundation it was contracted to be, exposing Marathon to both commercial overage and stranded platform capabilities.
Trigger a BTP contract re-balancing and true-up on Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement (LPR868) to reallocate the Joule Studio overconsumption against the unused Integration Suite and Technology Foundation balances, and resize the ceiling to reflect the actual AI workload trajectory.
Launch an Integration Suite activation plan to land the iFlows, APIs, and Event Mesh scenarios that justified the April ACV uplift, and pair it with a Joule Studio governance review to convert burst-mode AI consumption into a governed, agent-ready integration architecture.
Solution Area
Business Transformation Management
61.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$97K
YTD Budget$158K
YTD ACV: $181K
Projected Consumed: $234K · Projected Budget: $384K · Projected ACV: $434K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Business Transformation Management stack at Marathon is architecturally bifurcated — Process Intelligence (mining/discovery) is the dominant investment but is decelerating, while Process Management (modeling/governance) is just now accelerating, indicating the LoB is shifting from 'discovering how processes run' to 'designing how they should run' without a closed-loop tie back into mining-driven validation.
Signavio Process Management's ACV step-up to $9,222 signals contractual commitment to enterprise-wide process modeling, but consumption at $1,222/month shows the repository is being used by a small modeling cell rather than as a system of record for process ownership — the governance layer is contracted but not yet institutionalized across process owners.
Reconcile the -$29,700 May entry in Signavio Process Intelligence and rebaseline attainment, then redirect the $150,356 projected shortfall into expanding mining coverage into O2C and P2P so the discovery layer continues feeding the newly expanded modeling layer.
Operationalize Signavio Process Management by assigning named process owners to the expanded ACV capacity and linking published models back to Process Intelligence findings, converting the April–May modeling momentum into a sustained governance practice rather than isolated diagramming activity.
Solution Area
Cloud ERP Private
38.3%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$9K
YTD Budget$23K
YTD ACV: $27K
Projected Consumed: $21K · Projected Budget: $55K · Projected ACV: $65K
Solution Insights and Actions
Preferred Success is the advisory and enablement layer wrapped around the S/4HANA Private Cloud core — its halving run rate ($2,156 to $1,078/month) signals that Marathon's CoE enablement, release readiness, and continuous quality check entitlements are going untouched, leaving the underlying ERP transformation without its guardrails and value-realization scaffolding.
With only 15.8% of the $54,709 entitlement consumed and the trajectory pointing to ~30% full-year utilization, the architectural intent of Preferred Success — to accelerate time-to-value on S/4HANA Private Cloud through proactive guidance — is effectively dormant, meaning Marathon is operating the platform without the prescriptive advisory cadence it was contracted to receive.
Re-engage Marathon on the Preferred Success S/4HANA Private Cloud plan to rebuild monthly burn toward the ~$4,500 budget line and reactivate the advisory cadence that reversed in April.
Book a value-realization workshop to draw down the $46,085 unused Preferred Success entitlement into concrete engagements across CoE enablement, release/upgrade advisory, and continuous quality checks.

Projected Consumed: $1.0M · Projected Budget: $1.2M · Projected ACV: $1.7M
Solution Insights and Actions
Marathon's procurement architecture is operating in two disconnected modes — Ariba Buying and Invoicing is overshooting plan at 138.7% while Ariba Catalog declines monthly and Ariba Sourcing flatlines below 48%, meaning transactional P2P is absorbing volume that should have been pre-negotiated through Sourcing and channeled via Catalog, so the LoB is processing spend without upstream price discipline or compliance guardrails.
The Finance analytics stack is architecturally incomplete — SAC Planning is locked into a single recurring workload while the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement sits at zero consumption, leaving the LoB with a narrow planning process and no extensibility platform to unify financial and operational planning or deliver the AI-driven decision support the contract was sized for.
Re-route Ariba Buying and Invoicing's $58K YTD overage back through Ariba Sourcing and Ariba Catalog by identifying the unmanaged spend categories driving transactional volume and onboarding them as sourced, catalog-enabled flows to restore upstream governance.
Activate the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement by anchoring a first production workload that extends SAC Planning beyond its flat $3,833/month footprint — such as workforce or capex planning models — to unblock both the zero-consumption stall and the narrow planning adoption pattern.
Medtronic, Inc.
Life sciences and healthcare
YTD Consumed: $2.1M
YTD Budget: $2.6M
YTD ACV: $3.2M
Projected Consumed: $4.9M
Projected Budget: $6.4M
Projected ACV: $7.7M

Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
0.4%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$11
YTD Budget$3K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $26 · Projected Budget: $5K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
BTP Enterprise Agreement is architecturally positioned as Medtronic's foundation for AI-enabled extensions, integrations, and data pipelines across both Public and Database Cloud environments, yet both subaccounts show effectively zero consumption ($0 and $11 against a combined ~$3,086 YTD budget) — the platform that should be powering agentic development and data unification for Data & AI is contracted but completely dormant, leaving the LoB without the runtime, integration, or AI context layer that every downstream BTP-dependent capability assumes is in place.
The single $11 Database Cloud charge in May against four prior zero months indicates a sandbox touch rather than a production data workload, meaning Medtronic's Data & AI line of business has no operational data foundation on BTP — without persistent HANA Cloud or data lake consumption, any planned analytics, ML, or AI-with-context use cases cannot architecturally land, and the full ~$3.9K annual contract value trends toward shelfware.
Stand up a BTP activation workshop focused on identifying one production integration or extension workload (Integration Suite flow or Build extension) that moves LPR1153 Public consumption off $0 and establishes the runtime foundation the rest of the Data & AI portfolio depends on.
Convert the May $11 Database Cloud sandbox activity into a scoped data foundation pilot — provision HANA Cloud or a data lake instance tied to a named Data & AI use case — so the LoB has a persistent data layer capable of supporting AI-with-context scenarios rather than trial-grade activity.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
66.8%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$82K
YTD Budget$123K
YTD ACV: $159K
Projected Consumed: $197K · Projected Budget: $321K · Projected ACV: $380K
Solution Insights and Actions
The integration backbone is architecturally central but operationally regressing — SAP Integration Suite is the only active Technology Foundation product yet has declined three months running from $13.5K to $11.6K, signaling that Medtronic's API and iflow estate is contracting rather than expanding to support the AI-ready integration role the platform is designed to play.
BTP is contracted as the agentic AI, extension, and data foundation but is effectively dormant outside Joule Studio — two of three BTP Enterprise Agreement allocations (Integration Suite enablement and Technology Foundation) sit at $0 consumed, while Joule Studio alone is overshooting budget by $13.6K, meaning Medtronic is funding a broad platform but only activating a narrow AI development slice.
Launch a BTP activation workshop targeting the two zero-consumption LPR1153 allocations ($3,381 Integration Suite + $1,297 Technology Foundation) to stand up named extension, data, or integration workloads owned by Medtronic platform leads.
Run an Integration Suite reactivation review to diagnose the three-month consumption decline and seed net-new B2B, API, and event-driven scenarios that absorb the projected $132K of unused contract capacity while pairing a true-up on the Joule Studio overage.
Solution Area
Customer Experience
27.9%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$132K
YTD Budget$475K
YTD ACV: $890K
Projected Consumed: $318K · Projected Budget: $1.2M · Projected ACV: $2.1M
Solution Insights and Actions
Medtronic's commerce architecture is inverted — the advisory wrapper (Preferred Success Commerce) is over-consumed and hitting an ACV ceiling while the underlying SAP Commerce Cloud platform sits at 8.4% YTD attainment, meaning the LoB is paying for guidance on a platform it has barely deployed beyond an initial pilot workload.
SAP Commerce Cloud's core capabilities — multi-channel storefronts, catalog management, and unified commerce experiences — are effectively dormant at a flat $10,300/month run rate, leaving over $880K of committed ACV unrealized and indicating Medtronic has stood up the platform but not extended it to additional B2B catalogs, regional storefronts, or customer portals the architecture is designed to serve.
Rebalance the Preferred Success Commerce engagement model so advisory hours are explicitly redirected toward unlocking dormant SAP Commerce Cloud capabilities — catalog expansion, storefront rollouts, and promotions — rather than being rationed against a flat $19,999/month ceiling.
Launch a SAP Commerce Cloud adoption roadmap with Medtronic's digital commerce team to onboard additional B2B catalogs, regional storefronts, and customer portals, converting the $355,378 YTD budget gap and $907,179 remaining ACV into active transactional volume.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
91.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$1.8M
YTD Budget$2.0M
YTD ACV: $2.1M
Projected Consumed: $4.4M · Projected Budget: $4.9M · Projected ACV: $5.2M
Solution Insights and Actions
Medtronic's Finance and Spend Management architecture has a fully operational transactional layer (Ariba Buying, Buying and Invoicing, Concur Expense/Travel/ExpenseIt all at or above plan) but a completely dormant governance and risk layer — Watchlist Screening and Ariba Supplier Risk both sit at $0 consumed, meaning purchases and payments are flowing without sanctions screening or supplier risk monitoring, which leaves the LoB exposed to trade compliance, AML, and third-party risk events that the contracted architecture was specifically designed to prevent.
The sourcing-to-pay chain is architecturally unbalanced — Ariba Sourcing is decelerating toward a projected 44% shortfall while downstream Ariba Buying and Buying & Invoicing run at or above plan, indicating Medtronic is transacting heavily on agreements that were never strategically sourced through the platform, eroding the negotiated-savings and policy-compliance value that justifies the procurement suite.
Activate Watchlist Screening (LPR645) and Ariba Supplier Risk (LPR749) by wiring them into the live Ariba Buying and Buying & Invoicing transaction flows so that every supplier and payment passes through sanctions screening and risk profiling before release, closing the compliance control gap created by zero consumption on both products.
Rebalance the sourcing layer by launching an Ariba Sourcing (LPR638) category migration with Medtronic procurement to move high-spend categories currently bypassing the platform into RFx and e-auction workflows, restoring the strategic upstream that feeds Ariba Buying and protects the $52K downstream invoicing shortfall risk.

Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI foundation for Molson Coors is architecturally contracted but operationally dark — both BDC Public and Database Cloud tenants of SAP BTP Enterprise Agreement remain unactivated, meaning the LoB has no live substrate for agentic AI, integration, or data services despite owning the entitlement.
With $0 consumed against a combined $4,973 annual commitment across both BTP environments, the LoB is in a pre-adoption stall rather than a ramp curve, which signals that no first workload (integration, extension, or data product) has been architecturally anchored to justify tenant provisioning.
Launch a joint BTP activation workshop with Molson Coors' platform and architecture leads to designate a first production workload — integration suite or S/4HANA extension — that moves SAP BTP Enterprise Agreement consumption off zero on both BDC Public and Database Cloud.
Establish a named-use-case BTP consumption ramp plan covering both SAP BTP Enterprise Agreement tenants, binding the $4,973 contracted ceiling to specific data and AI workloads so entitlement converts into measurable monthly adoption.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
—
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$0
YTD Budget$0
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $0 · Projected Budget: $10K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
The entire BTP Enterprise Agreement is dormant across all three architectural pillars — Joule Studio (AI development), Integration Suite (process integration), and broader Technology Foundation services — meaning Molson Coors has contracted the full SAP extensibility, integration, and AI foundation but has not activated a single workload, leaving every downstream SAP application without the connective tissue BTP is meant to provide.
With $0 consumed against a combined $10,098 cumulative budget across Joule Studio, Integration Suite, and Technology Foundation, the LoB is operating SAP without its strategic AI and integration layer — any custom extensions, cross-system integrations, or Joule-based agentic AI use cases that should be running on BTP are either being built on non-SAP platforms or not being built at all, creating architectural drift from the SAP roadmap.
Launch a BTP Enterprise Agreement activation workshop anchored on Integration Suite as the first landed workload, since integration is the most common entry point and will unlock downstream Joule Studio and extension scenarios against the $10,098 dormant commitment.
Escalate the LPR1153 zero-consumption pattern to the account executive to confirm whether the BTP business case is still funded internally and assign a named technical sponsor at Molson Coors accountable for first-developer-login and subaccount provisioning.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
82.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$1.1M
YTD Budget$1.3M
YTD ACV: $1.3M
Projected Consumed: $2.6M · Projected Budget: $3.2M · Projected ACV: $3.2M
Solution Insights and Actions
The Source-to-Pay architecture is fully licensed but operating as disconnected modules — Ariba Sourcing (75% attainment), Contracts (62%), and Buying/Invoicing (73%) are all plateaued in the same 60-77% band, indicating Molson Coors is running each procurement stage in isolation rather than as a continuous sourced-to-contracted-to-purchased flow, which is precisely the integrated value the Ariba suite is architected to deliver.
The procurement intelligence and innovation layers are dormant while transactional Ariba burns flat — Spend Analysis is drifting downward (97.8% in March to 75.2% in May) and the BTP Enterprise Agreement sits at $0 consumed against $492 budget, meaning Molson Coors has no analytical feedback loop to identify off-platform spend and no extension platform to automate or AI-enable the procurement processes that Preferred Success Ariba is already being billed $41,357/month to advise on.
Launch a Source-to-Pay process integration play that ties Ariba Sourcing awards directly into Ariba Contracts authoring and Ariba Buying catalog enablement, converting the flat $15-18K sourcing run-rate and $8-10K contracts ceiling into a single governed pipeline that pulls under-routed spend back onto the Ariba Buying and Invoicing channel.
Redirect the passive $41,357/month Preferred Success Ariba entitlement burn into funded BTP activation workshops that stand up the first Spend Analysis-to-BTP extension use case, simultaneously addressing the $0 BTP consumption, the declining Spend Analysis attainment, and the projected $76,551 Preferred Success overrun.
Solution Area
Human Capital Management
90.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$778K
YTD Budget$864K
YTD ACV: $959K
Projected Consumed: $1.9M · Projected Budget: $2.1M · Projected ACV: $2.3M
Solution Insights and Actions
Employee Central is anchoring the HCM suite as the system of record but its flat consumption against rising budget shows it has settled into baseline personnel transactions — Position Management, MDF extensions, and global workflows that would feed downstream Talent, Learning, and Compensation modules are dormant, which explains why every connected SuccessFactors product is simultaneously plateauing.
The Talent suite (Career Development, Performance and Goals, Compensation, Learning) is architecturally wired together but operating as disconnected islands — flat Performance and Goals usage means no skills-linked performance signal flows into Career Development or Compensation, so merit planning is eroding, career worksheets sit unused, and Learning completions are not tied to skill growth, defeating the integrated talent value chain.
Preferred Success SFSF is being fully drawn down and trending 15.9% over its annual ceiling while every SuccessFactors module it supports is under-consuming — expert-led services are being consumed reactively rather than steered at the dormant capabilities (EC modules, talent integration, Compensation workflows) that are causing the suite-wide adoption plateau.
Run an Employee Central capability activation play to switch on Position Management, MDF extensions, and advanced workflows so the foundational data model can feed Career Development, Performance and Goals, and Compensation with the org and skills signal they need to scale beyond their current flat user base.
Redirect Preferred Success SFSF expert engagements toward a Talent suite integration sprint — wiring Performance and Goals outputs into Compensation planning worksheets and Career Development pathways, and Learning completions into skills profiles — to convert the $51K overrun into adoption lift across the four under-consuming Talent modules.

BAIP - Data & AI
96.9%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$276K
YTD Budget$285K
YTD ACV: $322K
Projected Consumed: $663K · Projected Budget: $637K · Projected ACV: $774K
Solution Insights and Actions
Mondelez's Data & AI architecture is anchored on analytics consumption but starved of the underlying data fabric — SAC BI is running at 98% attainment while Business Data Cloud sits at $0 consumed and Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement has flatlined since March, meaning the LoB is visualizing data without the modern data products, AI grounding, or platform compute that BDC and CPEA were contracted to provide.
The BI layer is showing fatigue precisely when the data foundation should be activating — SAC BI monthly consumption dropped 28% from Jan-Mar to Apr-May with $50K of ACV headroom unused, while BDC's entire $14,288 budget is back-loaded to a single December checkpoint, signaling no architectural plan to feed new data products or AI workloads into SAC to reignite engagement.
Launch a joint BDC and SAC BI activation workshop with Mondelez to scope the first Business Data Cloud data product that lands directly into SAC dashboards, converting the dormant $14,288 BDC commitment and $50K SAC ACV gap into a single connected analytics-on-data-fabric use case.
Investigate the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement consumption halt to determine whether the Jan-Mar database workloads were migrated, decommissioned, or stalled, and rebind the remaining $7,726 budget to compute that supports BDC and SAC workloads rather than leaving the platform layer orphaned from the analytics stack.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
108.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$201K
YTD Budget$185K
YTD ACV: $224K
Projected Consumed: $481K · Projected Budget: $403K · Projected ACV: $536K
Solution Insights and Actions
The integration backbone is architecturally load-bearing and operationally saturated — SAP Integration Suite is running 124% to budget with a projected $117,846 overrun, confirming it is the active conduit for Mondelez's API, data, and AI agent traffic and is on track to hit a hard capacity ceiling that will throttle dependent workloads.
The extension and AI platform layer is contracted but architecturally stranded — Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement saw Jan-Feb activity collapse into a May reversal and 54% projected attainment, meaning the Joule Studio and BTP-based extension capabilities meant to complement Integration Suite are not being built on, leaving integrations without the agents, extensions, or data services they were designed to feed.
Initiate a true-up on SAP Integration Suite to expand contracted capacity ahead of the $117,846 projected overrun and run a workload review to confirm which interfaces and message volumes are driving the sustained ~$37K/month run rate.
Investigate the -$467 May reversal on Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement to confirm whether a workload was decommissioned, and build an expansion plan that activates 2-3 net-new BTP services (extensions, data, or Joule agents) on top of the existing Integration Suite footprint to close the $39,865 budget gap.
Solution Area
Business Transformation Management
20.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$60K
YTD Budget$291K
YTD ACV: $250K
Projected Consumed: $144K · Projected Budget: $662K · Projected ACV: $601K
Solution Insights and Actions
Mondelez's Business Transformation Management architecture pairs Signavio Process Intelligence (process mining and conformance) with Signavio Process Management (process modeling and governance), but both are dormant — without active modeling to define the to-be process and active mining to measure the as-is, the LoB has no closed-loop transformation capability and is effectively running blind on process improvement despite owning the toolchain.
The Process Management stall at ~10.5% YTD attainment ($2,972 of $28,210) means no authoritative process repository exists to anchor Process Intelligence findings, so even if the $113K May credit on Process Intelligence is resolved, the LoB has no governed process models to mine against — leaving roughly $518K of combined annual budget ($585K + $77K) unrealized and the transformation program architecturally incoherent.
Resolve the SAP Signavio Process Intelligence May -$113,176 entry with finance and the account team to confirm whether it is a credit, true-up, or contract reset before any reactivation plan is committed.
Relaunch SAP Signavio Process Management and Process Intelligence together against 1–2 priority end-to-end processes (Order-to-Cash or Procure-to-Pay) with named process owners, modeler license activation, Collaboration Hub publishing, and a paired mining use case to close the $448K Process Intelligence and $69,744 Process Management budget gaps.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
86.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$499K
YTD Budget$578K
YTD ACV: $661K
Projected Consumed: $1.2M · Projected Budget: $1.3M · Projected ACV: $1.6M
Solution Insights and Actions
SAP Analytics Cloud Planning is architecturally positioned as a unified financial and operational planning platform, but Mondelez is consuming it as a static reporting surface — the flat $97K–$102K band against a $115K budget signals a fixed analyst cohort using it for plan visualization rather than exercising the cross-functional planning capabilities (workforce, OpEx, driver-based forecasting) the contract was sized for.
The $120K projected annual under-consumption against the $1.32M ceiling reveals a capability-to-process mismatch — the LoB has licensed an enterprise planning backbone but has not extended it beyond core FP&A, leaving Joule-assisted forecasting and operational planning integration dormant while finance continues to run adjacent planning cycles outside the platform.
Run a use-case expansion workshop on SAP Analytics Cloud Planning to onboard workforce, OpEx, and driver-based forecasting scenarios, converting the ~$15K monthly shortfall into active planning workloads.
Execute a license utilization audit on SAP Analytics Cloud Planning to identify passive viewers versus active plan contributors, and re-enable dormant seats as planners to lift consumption above the $116K monthly budget line.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
100.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$821K
YTD Budget$821K
YTD ACV: $821K
Projected Consumed: $2.0M · Projected Budget: $2.0M · Projected ACV: $2.0M
Solution Insights and Actions
Mondelez's Supply Chain Management architecture is split between an active planning layer and a dormant product development layer — IBP is running steady-state at full budget attainment while SAP Integrated Product Development sits at zero consumption, meaning demand and supply planning operate without an upstream connection to new product introduction, cost engineering, or PLM-driven specifications.
IBP's flat $164,268/month consumption signals that only a subset of planning modules (likely Demand or S&OP) is operational while Response, Supply, and Inventory Optimization capabilities appear dormant — the LoB has a unified planning platform contracted but is running it as a single-process tool, which will not absorb the Nov–Dec budget step-up to $194,559 and leaves a $51,121 annual gap.
Activate SAP Integrated Product Development by anchoring it to an R&D-to-supply-chain handover use case (formula or specification management) with a named Mondelez executive sponsor to close the $4,601 non-adoption gap and connect PLM into the supply chain architecture.
Conduct an IBP module footprint review to identify dormant capabilities (Response, Supply, Inventory Optimization) and activate at least one to absorb the Q4 budget escalation and convert the $51,121 projected shortfall into realized planning value.

Finance and Spend Management
78.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$54K
YTD Budget$69K
YTD ACV: $85K
Projected Consumed: $131K · Projected Budget: $168K · Projected ACV: $204K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Travel & Expense architecture is fragmented — Concur Expense (the system of record for submissions) collapsed to -$5 in May with a -$1,440 ACV adjustment, yet adjacent capabilities like ExpenseIt (receipt capture), Intelligent Audit (policy enforcement), and Reporting Services are all over-consuming, meaning the LoB is scanning, auditing, and reporting on a submission pipeline that has effectively stopped flowing.
Statutory compliance is contractually provisioned but architecturally inert — Document & Reporting Compliance public is frozen at ~$40/month (5% projected attainment) while Concur Expense and Company Bill Statement (the upstream transactional feeds it depends on for e-invoicing and tax reporting) are also degrading, leaving the Ingredients Americas LoB exposed on VAT and e-document obligations across its operating countries.
Restore the Concur Expense submission backbone by investigating the May -$5 consumption and -$1,440 ACV reversal with AP/Finance, re-enabling expense workflows so the $19,271 projected shortfall and the dependent ExpenseIt, Intelligent Audit, and Reporting Services investments remain anchored to a live transaction stream.
Activate Document & Reporting Compliance public beyond its single dormant use case by scoping additional country/legal entity statutory filings and e-invoicing flows, converting the $8,869 of unrealized contract value into operational compliance coverage.
S.C. Johnson & Son, Inc.
Consumer products
YTD Consumed: $600K
YTD Budget: $735K
YTD ACV: $1.0M
Projected Consumed: $1.4M
Projected Budget: $1.8M
Projected ACV: $2.4M
EA Insights and Actions
S.C. Johnson has operationalized consumption layers across every L1 domain — SAP Business Data Cloud at 2.7x, Concur capture tools, Blackline reconciliations, and Business Network collaboration — while the underlying BTP substrate (Cloud Platform EA, Joule Studio, broader Integration Suite scenarios) sits dormant, meaning the enterprise is running mission-critical Finance, Supply Chain, and Data workloads on a foundation it has paid for but not activated, blocking the agentic, AI-governed, clean-core architecture the contract envelope was sized to deliver.
Integration Suite is the single common dependency underpinning the broken value chains in both Finance (Concur Expense collapse to 21% with upstream ExpenseIt/Triplink still active) and Supply Chain (SAP Integrated Product Development at zero post-March severing PLM-to-S/4-to-Business Network flow), yet it is being run as a project-burst iPaaS at a flat $3.5K/month rather than a governed integration fabric — meaning closed-loop T&E reimbursement and design-to-supply continuity will keep fracturing at every domain handoff until Integration Suite is operationalized as enterprise middleware.
The portfolio shows a consistent pattern of contracted-but-under-consumed foundational layers (Cloud Platform EA dormant, Joule Studio near-zero, IPD at 5.6%, Business Network tracking $130K short, Blackline $75K under ACV) coexisting with one runaway consumption line (BDC at 210%+), indicating S.C. Johnson's enterprise architecture is commercially imbalanced — over-indexed on a single AI/data workload while the integration, extensibility, and process-automation entitlements that justify the broader SAP investment thesis remain unrealized, putting renewal-cycle value defense at risk across multiple L1s simultaneously.
Convene an enterprise architecture review across BAIP, Finance, and Supply Chain leadership to rebaseline the BDC overage against the dormant Cloud Platform EA, Joule Studio, and broader BTP entitlements, and produce a single activation roadmap that lands the foundation layer beneath the operationalized consumption.
Stand up an Integration Suite enterprise fabric workshop scoping API management, event mesh, and B2B patterns specifically for the Concur Expense-to-ExpenseIt/Triplink chain and the IPD-to-S/4-to-Business Network chain, converting project-burst usage into the governed integration backbone that closes both broken cross-domain value chains.
Initiate a portfolio-level commercial and adoption rebalancing review covering the BDC ACV uplift case, the $37K BTP shortfall, the $34K Concur gap, the $75K Blackline gap, and the $129K Business Network gap, to realign contracted entitlements with the customer's actual architectural center of gravity ahead of renewal.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
189.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$47K
YTD Budget$25K
YTD ACV: $154K
Projected Consumed: $112K · Projected Budget: $73K · Projected ACV: $369K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI architecture at S.C. Johnson is operating in a split-brain state — SAP Business Data Cloud is being consumed at 2.7x the planned envelope while the underlying Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement (both BDC Public and Database Cloud entitlements) sits fully dormant, meaning the LoB is running mission-critical data workloads without the contracted platform substrate that was meant to underpin them.
The agentic AI and business data fabric capabilities of SAP Business Data Cloud are clearly operationalized given the sustained overage, but the dormant Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement entitlements indicate the LoB has not extended into the broader platform services intended to scale and govern that fabric, leaving the architecture top-heavy on consumption and thin on foundation.
Rebaseline the SAP Business Data Cloud commercial envelope to reflect the operationalized run rate and determine whether the 210%+ attainment represents high-value expansion warranting an ACV uplift or workload patterns requiring tuning.
Convene a Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement activation workshop covering both BDC Public and Database Cloud entitlements to land the first production workloads and align the dormant platform layer with the active SAP Business Data Cloud consumption it is meant to support.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
70.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$19K
YTD Budget$26K
YTD ACV: $21K
Projected Consumed: $45K · Projected Budget: $84K · Projected ACV: $51K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Technology Foundation layer is architecturally contracted but functionally hollow — Integration Suite is the only active capability while Joule Studio and broader BTP services sit at near-zero consumption, meaning S.C. Johnson has bought a platform for AI-ready integration, extension, and agent enablement but is operating it as a single-purpose iPaaS.
Integration Suite usage itself is event-driven rather than embedded — the sawtooth consumption pattern and flat $3.5K/month Enterprise Agreement footprint indicate APIs are being built for discrete projects rather than operationalized as the governed, AI-consumable integration fabric the architecture is designed to deliver, capping the LoB's ability to scale agentic and process automation use cases.
Stand up a Joule Studio activation workshop on the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement to deploy a first production agent use case, converting the dormant $1,935 LPR868 commitment into a foundational AI extension workload.
Expand SAP Integration Suite beyond the current single-workload baseline by onboarding API management, B2B, or event mesh scenarios to close the combined ~$37K projected shortfall across LPR868 and LPR499 and shift adoption from project bursts to recurring operational consumption.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
90.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$300K
YTD Budget$333K
YTD ACV: $427K
Projected Consumed: $721K · Projected Budget: $810K · Projected ACV: $1.0M
Solution Insights and Actions
S.C. Johnson's Travel & Expense stack is architecturally complete — booking (Direct Travel), capture (ExpenseIt), itinerary reconciliation (Triplink), submission (Expense), analytics, and reporting all show active adoption — yet the core Concur Expense submission engine collapsed to 21% attainment in May, breaking the T&E value chain because receipts and trips are being captured upstream but not converted into reimbursable, policy-governed expense reports downstream.
The Record-to-Report architecture is partially activated — Blackline is operating at 92% YTD but trending $75K below ACV with event-driven monthly volatility, meaning S.C. Johnson is using it as a periodic reconciliation tool rather than the continuous close-automation backbone it is designed to be, leaving controllership dependent on manual effort that the platform is contracted to eliminate.
Diagnose the May Concur Expense collapse with S.C. Johnson's AP/Finance lead to confirm whether submitter cohorts, approval workflows, or integration to ExpenseIt/Triplink broke, and reactivate dormant submitters to recover the $13,531 YTD and $34,309 projected annual gap.
Expand Blackline Account Substantiation scope by onboarding additional GL accounts and reconciling entities, and enforce standardized templates and auto-certification rules with the controllership team to close the $75,702 ACV gap and convert event-driven usage into embedded close discipline.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
66.8%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$234K
YTD Budget$351K
YTD ACV: $417K
Projected Consumed: $562K · Projected Budget: $846K · Projected ACV: $1.0M
Solution Insights and Actions
The supply chain collaboration backbone is contracted but under-leveraged — Bus Netw Supply Chain Collaboration is live yet tracking to only ~$550K of $680K annual budget with a May volume drop, meaning S.C. Johnson has the network rails for forecast, PO, and ASN collaboration but is not extending them across enough trading partners to realize resiliency and visibility outcomes.
The new product introduction bridge has collapsed — SAP Integrated Product Development went to zero consumption after March at 5.6% YTD attainment, severing the architectural link between PLM, S/4HANA manufacturing, and procurement and leaving product designs without supply chain feasibility or cost alignment, which also weakens the upstream demand signals that feed the Business Network.
Launch a supplier onboarding and use-case expansion sprint on Bus Netw Supply Chain Collaboration covering order, invoice, ASN, and forecast collaboration flows to broaden the active trading partner footprint and recover the projected $129K annual shortfall.
Re-engage product development stakeholders to diagnose the post-March abandonment of SAP Integrated Product Development and rebuild activation around formulation, recipe, and cost-to-design capabilities to reconnect engineering with manufacturing and procurement.
Stepan Company
Chemicals
YTD Consumed: $506K
YTD Budget: $672K
YTD ACV: $650K
Projected Consumed: $1.2M
Projected Budget: $1.7M
Projected ACV: $1.6M
EA Insights and Actions
No insights available
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
0.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$0
YTD Budget$3K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $0 · Projected Budget: $9K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
Stepan's entire BAIP Data & AI foundation is architecturally contracted but operationally inert — Business Data Cloud, BDC Public, and Database Cloud all sit at $0 consumption, meaning the data fabric, analytics, and AI-ready storage layers that should anchor the LoB's intelligent data strategy have never been activated.
Without Business Data Cloud onboarded, the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement entitlements for BDC Public and Database Cloud have no consuming workload to attach to — the LoB has procured the compute and storage substrate but not the data products or Datasphere/SAC surfaces that would generate consumption, leaving a combined $7,434 in annual commitments ($2,930 YTD gap) exposed to forfeiture.
Stand up a unified BAIP Data & AI activation workshop anchored on SAP Business Data Cloud to define the first data product and consuming analytics or AI workload, since none of the three dormant contracts will move off $0 until a concrete use case is selected.
Sequence Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement activation for BDC Public and Database Cloud directly behind the first Business Data Cloud data product onboarding so the infrastructure entitlements consume against a real workload rather than remaining dormant capacity.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
81.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$23K
YTD Budget$28K
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $56K · Projected Budget: $69K · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
The Technology Foundation is architecturally fragmented across three distinct consumption streams (Joule Studio, Integration Suite, and other BTP services) but all three are simultaneously contracting — Joule Studio is capped at a sub-60% footprint, Integration Suite has declined 17% since January, and the residual BTP slice is shrinking month-over-month, indicating Stepan has stopped treating BTP as a strategic platform and is instead running it as a static set of point workloads.
The Integration Suite trajectory is the most damaging architectural signal — as the connective tissue between SAP and non-SAP systems, its declining run rate (from $3,957 to $3,278) means integration flows are being retired or migrated off-platform, which will starve downstream extension, analytics, and AI use cases that depend on Integration Suite as their data backbone, explaining why Joule Studio adoption has plateaued in parallel.
Launch an Integration Suite consumption recovery review with Stepan to identify which iFlows or APIs drove the five-month decline and reactivate or replace those workloads before the $5,333 annual shortfall compounds into renewal downsizing.
Run a joint BTP use-case discovery workshop pairing Joule Studio with Integration Suite to land 2–3 net-new AI-plus-integration workloads that absorb the combined ~$8,000 projected unconsumed budget and re-establish BTP as an expanding platform rather than a capped footprint.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
69.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$292K
YTD Budget$421K
YTD ACV: $396K
Projected Consumed: $700K · Projected Budget: $1.1M · Projected ACV: $950K
Solution Insights and Actions
Stepan's source-to-pay architecture is fracturing in the middle — Ariba Sourcing (5.2% attainment, declining) and Ariba Catalog (0.5% attainment, dormant) are functionally inactive while Ariba Buying and Invoicing carries the operational load alone, meaning purchases are flowing without strategic sourcing events upstream or catalog-enforced compliance at the point of requisition, eroding the spend governance the Ariba investment was meant to deliver.
The Travel & Expense stack is bifurcating — analytics, reporting, ExpenseIt capture, and integration services are all running over budget and operationally embedded, yet Concur Expense itself collapsed to 27.9% attainment in May and Intelligent Audit oscillates at 54–72%, indicating the supporting capabilities are healthy while the core expense submission and audit control layer is losing transaction volume, leaving Stepan with strong visibility into a shrinking pool of governed expense activity.
Reconnect the Ariba source-to-pay chain by launching a catalog enablement sprint on Ariba Catalog for the top indirect spend categories and resetting the Ariba Sourcing event pipeline with category managers, so requisitions in Ariba Buying and Invoicing are fed by negotiated contracts and catalog content rather than off-platform decisions.
Stabilize the Concur Expense core by investigating the March-to-May submission drop-off in Concur Expense and enabling continuous (not sampled) audit coverage in Concur Intelligent Audit, restoring the transactional foundation that Concur Analytics, Reporting Services, and ExpenseIt depend on for value.
Solution Area
Human Capital Management
87.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$191K
YTD Budget$220K
YTD ACV: $255K
Projected Consumed: $460K · Projected Budget: $533K · Projected ACV: $611K
Solution Insights and Actions
The core HCM foundation is eroding — SF Employee Central consumption is declining while budget rises, meaning the system of record that feeds Payroll, Talent, Learning, and Performance is losing active engagement and weakening every downstream module's data quality.
The talent development loop is architecturally complete but operationally hollow — Employee Central, Performance and Goals, and Career and Talent Development are all contracted, yet Performance attainment is sliding to 76% and Career Development is tracking to ~51%, so skills, goals, and growth conversations are not flowing through the integrated talent stack as designed.
Payroll consolidation is only half-realized — SF Employee Central Payroll is consuming ~54% of contracted ACV with a $27,110 projected annual shortfall, indicating entire legal entities or payroll populations remain off-platform and the 'single HR + payroll cloud' architecture is not actually unified.
Run an Employee Central process audit to pinpoint which core HR workflows (employee data, org management, self-service) are being bypassed and rebuild adoption around the foundational record that anchors every other SuccessFactors module.
Drive a payroll scope completion plan for SF Employee Central Payroll covering off-platform legal entities, off-cycle, retro, and bonus runs to close the $11,814 YTD gap and bring the full employee population into the integrated HR-payroll cloud.
Target Corporation
Retail
YTD Consumed: $2.6M
YTD Budget: $2.4M
YTD ACV: $3.1M
Projected Consumed: $6.2M
Projected Budget: $5.8M
Projected ACV: $7.5M
EA Insights and Actions
Target has contracted a coherent intelligent-enterprise blueprint — BDC as the data fabric, Integration Suite as the API spine, Build/Joule as the extension layer, and Ariba/Concur/SCM as the transactional domains — but only the integration spine and transactional edges are live, meaning the customer is moving data and money at scale across a foundation that cannot govern, contextualize, or intelligently act on any of it.
Every L1 domain shows the same architectural signature: the execution-layer SaaS is over-consuming (Ariba Buying 121%, Concur stack 105–130%, Integration Suite 129%, Preferred Success SCM 113%) while the governance, planning, and platform layers beneath them (BDC $0, CPEA $0, Multi Bank Connect 15%, Ariba Contracts 67%, Build 15%, IBP/EWM/TM absent) are dormant — Target is running a retail enterprise on transactional throughput without the upstream control planes that make that throughput strategic.
For a retailer whose competitive thesis depends on demand-sensing, supplier orchestration, and unified merchandising-to-payment flows, the combination of a dormant Business Data Cloud, an orphaned Preferred Success SCM with no execution products, and a Multi Bank Connect gap leaves Target without the closed loop between forecast, source, contract, pay, and settle — the AI-and-agentic future the contract envisions has no substrate to run on.
Convene a cross-domain Enterprise Architecture review anchored on BDC + Integration Suite CPEA activation, treating the dormant BTP foundation as the single largest blocker to Data & AI, P2P governance, and SCM execution value realization.
Launch a P2P-to-Treasury closed-loop architecture workshop that ties the Ariba Buying overage and Concur over-attainment to Ariba Contracts/Sourcing governance upstream and Multi Bank Connect execution downstream, converting ungoverned transactional spend into a sourced, contracted, bank-settled flow.
Commission a Supply Chain execution-layer roadmap session using the Preferred Success SCM advisory consumption as the diagnostic, landing IBP or Ariba as the first live SCM product so the advisory spend and the broader S/4HANA-plus-BTP architecture finally converge on a deployed capability.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
87.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$60K
YTD Budget$69K
YTD ACV: $97K
Projected Consumed: $145K · Projected Budget: $173K · Projected ACV: $232K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Data & AI foundation is architecturally inverted at Target — SAP Business Data Cloud, the business data fabric meant to anchor AI and feed downstream analytics, sits at $0 against its $27,840 commitment, while SAC BI consumes data products it was designed to surface from BDC, leaving the LoB with visualization on legacy sources and no path to the AI-ready data fabric the architecture promises.
Within the analytics layer itself, SAC BI's two-month attainment slide from 94.4% to 80.3% and the $84,805 remaining gap signal that even the active capability is losing ground — the LoB is operating BI in steady-state decay rather than expanding toward the unified analytics-and-planning role SAC is contracted to play, meaning planning, Joule-driven decisioning, and business-context analytics remain dormant adjacent capabilities.
Convene a Business Data Cloud activation workshop with Target's data platform owners to unblock first workload onboarding and stand up one high-value data product, converting the dormant fabric into the foundation SAC BI and future agentic AI are meant to consume.
Run an SAC BI usage audit to identify the dashboards and user groups lost between March and April, then pair recovery with an expansion play into planning or Joule-enabled analytics to lift run-rate above the $12,115/month pace required to close the budget gap.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
76.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$65K
YTD Budget$86K
YTD ACV: $380K
Projected Consumed: $157K · Projected Budget: $253K · Projected ACV: $911K
Solution Insights and Actions
The integration backbone is the only architecturally active component in Target's BTP Technology Foundation — SAP Integration Suite is running at 129%+ attainment while the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement underpinning it sits at $0 consumption, meaning Target is operating integration workloads without the broader platform services (extension, governance, observability) the CPEA was contracted to enable.
The low-code and AI-extensibility layer is contracted but not operationalized — SAP Build sits at 15% attainment and Joule Studio CPEA consumption is declining month-over-month, so Target has no functioning channel to extend Integration Suite APIs into citizen-developed apps or agent-based experiences, leaving the 'AI-ready API' value of Integration Suite stranded at the integration tier.
Trigger an activation workshop for the SAP Integration Suite CPEA (LPR868) to provision the dormant BTP services that complement the live Integration Suite tenant, converting the $32,058 zero-consumption gap into governed extension and runtime capacity.
Scale the May SAP Build activation spike by pairing citizen-developer enablement with concrete extension use cases on top of the high-consumption Integration Suite APIs, reversing the Joule Studio CPEA decline and anchoring Build adoption to a proven integration backbone.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
109.1%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$2.4M
YTD Budget$2.2M
YTD ACV: $2.6M
Projected Consumed: $5.8M · Projected Budget: $5.2M · Projected ACV: $6.2M
Solution Insights and Actions
Target's Procure-to-Pay architecture is operationally lopsided — Ariba Buying and Invoicing is running 121% hot with a projected $575K overage while Ariba Contracts sits at 67% with volatile authoring and Ariba Sourcing is locked to a single $466/month recurring event, meaning Target is transacting and paying suppliers at scale without the upstream sourcing competition and contract governance that should be shaping those commitments.
The Travel & Expense stack is architecturally complete and broadly over-attaining (Concur Expense, Analytics, ExpenseIt, Drive, Direct Travel, Company Bill Statement, Connectors, plus WalkMe and Preferred Success all 105–130%), but the Treasury layer beneath it is dormant — Multi Bank Connect at 15% YTD with a May reversal signals Target has digitized employee-initiated spend capture without closing the loop to bank payment execution and statement retrieval from S/4HANA Treasury.
Launch a P2P rebalancing play that pairs an Ariba Buying and Invoicing contract true-up against the projected $575K overage with a categorical expansion of Ariba Sourcing events and an Ariba Contracts migration sprint, so transactional spend is governed by sourced, contracted terms rather than ungoverned invoice throughput.
Commission a Multi Bank Connect activation workstream with Target Treasury to onboard additional bank connections and payment formats — and resolve the May negative consumption/ACV adjustment — so the Concur-driven spend and AP volume flowing through Ariba Invoicing terminates in a governed, multi-bank payment and statement hub rather than point-to-point bank integrations.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
114.2%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$48K
YTD Budget$42K
YTD ACV: $48K
Projected Consumed: $115K · Projected Budget: $102K · Projected ACV: $115K
Solution Insights and Actions
Preferred Success SCM is the only active signal in Target's Supply Chain architecture, meaning the LoB is consuming SAP's guided adoption and advisory wrapper without any visible underlying SCM execution products (IBP, Ariba, EWM, TM) registering consumption — the success service is architecturally orphaned, advising on capabilities that aren't showing up as deployed workloads.
The flat $9,576/month draw producing 113% projected attainment ($114,917 vs $101,886 budget) confirms Target is fully metabolizing its entitlement of expert guidance, but because Preferred Success is a value-acceleration layer rather than a transactional SCM capability, this over-consumption signals unmet demand for prescriptive guidance on SCM transformation rather than proof of SCM process maturity.
Run a Preferred Success SCM value realization review with Target's SCM leadership to map which SCM products the advisory hours are being spent on and expose the execution-layer gap behind the steady consumption.
Use the $13,031 over-budget signal on LPR892 to position an expansion into an actual SCM execution product (IBP for planning or Ariba for procurement) so the advisory investment lands on a live capability rather than continuing to advise on a dormant architecture.
Tate and Lyle Americas LLC
Consumer products
YTD Consumed: $109K
YTD Budget: $135K
YTD ACV: $173K
Projected Consumed: $261K
Projected Budget: $330K
Projected ACV: $416K
EA Insights and Actions
Tate and Lyle's enterprise architecture shows a single-domain footprint concentrated entirely in Finance and Spend Management T&E, with no visible Supply Chain, HCM, or Customer Experience L1 presence — meaning the SAP estate is functioning as a point solution rather than an integrated digital core, leaving the ingredients manufacturer without the cross-domain data spine needed to connect spend, production, and customer outcomes.
The statutory compliance backbone (Document & Reporting Compliance) sits at <0.4% attainment while Concur processes high-volume multi-jurisdiction T&E spend, creating an architectural disconnect where the transactional layer is live but the governance layer is dormant — a material risk for a global specialty foods business operating across US, EMEA, and APAC tax regimes.
Preferred Success Concur is under-consumed at 70–82% precisely while its target remediation surfaces (Concur Expense collapsing to 30.6%, ExpenseIt, Direct Travel) are failing to attain — the customer owns the enablement architecture to self-heal adoption but the closed-loop between entitlement and outcome is broken, forfeiting both ACV and the operational value the stack was sized to deliver.
Commission an enterprise architecture review to map Tate and Lyle's full SAP footprint beyond T&E and identify whether Supply Chain, Manufacturing, and Sustainability domains are architecturally absent or running outside SAP, since a food ingredients leader cannot achieve traceability and margin objectives on a Finance-only SAP core.
Run a compliance architecture workshop to activate at least one e-invoicing or statutory reporting flow on Document & Reporting Compliance public, converting the dormant $8,837 budget into a live governance capability tied to the jurisdictions where Concur spend is being processed.
Reallocate unused Preferred Success Concur entitlements directly against the Concur Expense submission collapse and Direct Travel adoption gap, and concurrently true-up Company Bill Statement to resolve the $1,389 overage from sustained 116–129% consumption.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
80.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$109K
YTD Budget$135K
YTD ACV: $173K
Projected Consumed: $261K · Projected Budget: $330K · Projected ACV: $416K
Solution Insights and Actions
The Travel and Expense architecture is fully deployed end-to-end — Concur Expense, ExpenseIt, Direct Travel, Triplink, Intelligent Audit, Reporting Services, and User Support Desk are all active — but every component except Company Bill Statement is under-consuming against budget, indicating the stack is technically live but operationally under-leveraged across the traveler and expense submitter population.
Statutory compliance is architecturally contracted but functionally dormant — Document & Reporting Compliance public sits at <0.4% attainment while the Concur ecosystem runs at scale, meaning Tate and Lyle is processing high-volume T&E spend without activating the e-invoicing and statutory reporting backbone intended to govern that spend across jurisdictions.
Concur Expense, the transactional heart of the T&E architecture, is collapsing — May attainment fell to 30.6% following April's 61%, a two-month downward trend that signals upstream submission cadence is breaking down and will starve dependent capabilities (ExpenseIt capture, Intelligent Audit review, Reporting Services analytics) of the transaction volume they need to deliver value.
Preferred Success Concur is under-consumed at 70–82% attainment despite the broader Concur estate showing adoption gaps that Preferred Success is designed to remediate — the customer owns the enablement vehicle to fix Expense, ExpenseIt, and Direct Travel adoption but is not pulling on it.
Activate at least one statutory e-document or VAT reporting flow on Document & Reporting Compliance public to convert the dormant $8,837 remaining budget into a live compliance capability rather than forfeited ACV.
Diagnose the April–May Concur Expense submission drop-off by cost center and employee group, then re-enable mobile capture, delegate setup, and auto-submission rules to restore transaction flow into the dependent ExpenseIt, Intelligent Audit, and Reporting Services layers.
Redirect unused Preferred Success Concur entitlements (Expert Sessions, Empowerment workshops, release guidance) toward the specific adoption gaps in Concur Expense, Direct Travel, and Reporting Services to close the $2,578 YTD service gap with targeted enablement.
Initiate a contract true-up on Concur Company Bill Statement to right-size the $1,389 projected overage, since sustained 116–129% consumption reflects under-scoped sizing rather than an adoption lever to pull.
Tenneco Automotive
Automotive
YTD Consumed: $596K
YTD Budget: $780K
YTD ACV: $966K
Projected Consumed: $1.4M
Projected Budget: $1.8M
Projected ACV: $2.3M
EA Insights and Actions
Tenneco has contracted a full-stack SAP estate spanning HCM, CX, SCM, Finance, and Data & AI, but the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement — the connective tissue meant to integrate all of them — sits at $0 consumption across every LoB tenant, meaning the enterprise is operating as a federation of disconnected SaaS islands rather than the unified intelligent enterprise its ~$700K+ contract portfolio was designed to deliver.
The integration backbone is architecturally incoherent — Integration Suite runs at only 24% YTD attainment with erratic project-driven spikes while its CPEA entitlement sits fully dormant, which directly explains why C4C ($5,217 YTD unconsumed), IBP (frozen at single-module $1,826/month), and Employee Central (stalled at $49K/month feeding starved Talent modules) cannot exchange the master data needed to close the lead-to-cash, plan-to-deliver, and hire-to-retire loops critical to a tier-1 automotive supplier.
The HCM domain reveals the clearest pattern of the broader enterprise failure mode — Employee Central as the foundational data layer is under-populated, causing cascading erosion across Recruiting (83% flatlined), Onboarding (12%), Career Development (15%), and Learning — and this same 'foundation-starves-downstream' pathology is repeating in Data & AI (dormant BTP starving analytics), Finance (dormant CPEA starving FP&A extensions), and SCM (partial IBP starving integrated S&OP), indicating Tenneco lacks an enterprise architecture function enforcing foundation-first sequencing.
Commission an enterprise-wide BTP activation review across all five LPR868 CPEA tenants (Data & AI, Technology Foundation, Finance, CX, Integration) to designate named technical owners and convert the aggregate dormant platform entitlement into a single governed integration and extension substrate before further LoB workloads are deployed.
Launch a cross-domain integration architecture workshop anchored on Integration Suite to design the EC-to-C4C-to-IBP master data and event flows that close the hire-to-retire, lead-to-cash, and plan-to-deliver loops, redirecting the $9,166 Integration Suite ACV gap and dormant CPEA Integration slice into a managed API and event-mesh factory.
Establish an enterprise foundation-first adoption sequencing program with Tenneco that gates downstream module activation (Talent, FP&A extensions, IBP response planning, C4C service) on demonstrated foundation readiness in Employee Central, BTP, and Integration Suite to prevent the recurring pattern of starved downstream consumption across every L1 domain.
Solution Areas
Solution Area
BAIP - Data & AI
0.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$0
YTD Budget$41
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $0 · Projected Budget: $71 · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
Tenneco's Data & AI foundation is contractually established but architecturally inert — both BDC Public and Database Cloud instances of the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement sit at $0 consumption, meaning the LoB has no active BTP runtime, no data services in production, and no platform on which downstream integration, extensibility, or AI workloads can be built.
The dual-tenant CPEA footprint (BDC Public + Database Cloud) signals an intended separation between application-tier innovation and persistent data services, but with neither tenant activated the LoB is operating without the data plane needed to support analytics, AI, or HANA-based workloads — leaving roughly $71 of combined annual entitlement at risk of forfeiture against only ~$10/month of remaining runway needed across both contracts.
Convene a joint CPEA activation workshop covering both LPR868 tenants to identify one production workload per tenant — a BTP integration or extension use case on BDC Public and a HANA Cloud or data tiering use case on Database Cloud — and move both from zero to an initial billable footprint.
Build a backloaded, sequenced consumption ramp across both CPEA tenants that pairs Database Cloud data services with BDC Public application workloads so the platform is adopted as an integrated Data & AI stack rather than two isolated, under-utilized contracts.
Solution Area
BAIP - Technology Foundation
37.6%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$4K
YTD Budget$10K
YTD ACV: $10K
Projected Consumed: $9K · Projected Budget: $24K · Projected ACV: $23K
Solution Insights and Actions
The integration backbone is architecturally present but operationally fragmented — SAP Integration Suite shows erratic project-driven consumption ($102 baseline spiking to $571 in April, dropping to $374 in May) tracking only 24% of YTD budget, meaning Tenneco is treating it as a tactical iflow tool rather than the AI-ready API governance layer it is designed to be, leaving agent integration, prebuilt content, and 50% runtime cost reduction capabilities dormant.
BTP entitlements are split-personality across the estate — the Integration Suite slice of the Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement sits at $0 consumed while the Technology Foundation slice runs 109.5% over budget with 168-172% March/April spikes, signaling unmanaged project consumption on one side and fully shelfwared integration entitlements on the other, with no platform governance reconciling the two.
Consolidate the Integration Suite roadmap by activating the dormant $51 LPR868 Integration Suite entitlement and converting SAP Integration Suite's $9,166 ACV gap into a managed API and event-mesh factory anchored on S/4HANA, SuccessFactors, and supplier B2B scenarios.
Establish BTP consumption governance on Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement to reconcile the $1,874 projected Technology Foundation overrun against the $6,032 Joule Studio underrun, redirecting unconsumed entitlements toward sanctioned application development and analytics workloads.
Solution Area
Customer Experience
0.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$0
YTD Budget$5K
YTD ACV: $11K
Projected Consumed: $0 · Projected Budget: $13K · Projected ACV: $26K
Solution Insights and Actions
Cloud for Customer is architecturally positioned as the front-office system of engagement for sales and service execution, yet zero consumption against $5,217 YTD budget confirms the capability is contracted but never activated — Tenneco's Customer Experience LoB is operating without the digital sales backbone it has paid for.
With $10,895 of ACV already loaded and $12,693 remaining to be absorbed in the back half of the year, the C4C investment has shifted from a ramp risk to a value forfeiture scenario, leaving account, opportunity, and service processes running on whatever legacy or manual tooling the LoB has fallen back on.
Commission a Cloud for Customer go-live readiness diagnostic with Tenneco to pinpoint whether the blocker is tenant provisioning, identity integration, or sales team enablement, since none of these have produced a single transaction.
Anchor a Cloud for Customer adoption sprint on a single high-volume use case such as opportunity management for one sales region to establish baseline consumption and create a replicable rollout pattern for the remaining contract value.
Solution Area
Finance and Spend Management
0.0%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$0
YTD Budget$4
YTD ACV: $0
Projected Consumed: $0 · Projected Budget: $7 · Projected ACV: $0
Solution Insights and Actions
The Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement is the foundational entitlement that underpins extension, integration, and data services for Finance and Spend Management workloads — its complete non-consumption means Tenneco's finance LoB has no active platform substrate for custom analytics, workflow extensions, or integration scenarios that modern FP&A architectures depend on.
With zero consumption against a $4 YTD and $7 annual budget entering month 5, this is not a ramp problem but an architectural non-start — the LoB is operating its financial planning capability without the platform layer that was contracted to enable it, suggesting either a missing technical owner or that planning workloads were deployed without leveraging the entitled platform services.
Convene an activation workshop for Cloud Platform Enterprise Agreement (LPR868) with Tenneco's finance and platform owners to identify the first FP&A extension or integration workload and convert the dormant $7 entitlement into a live use case.
Establish a named technical owner and monthly consumption governance for LPR868 to close the $4 YTD gap and prevent the remaining 7 months from compounding into a full-year shelfware outcome.
Solution Area
Human Capital Management
77.7%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$583K
YTD Budget$750K
YTD ACV: $927K
Projected Consumed: $1.4M · Projected Budget: $1.7M · Projected ACV: $2.2M
Solution Insights and Actions
Tenneco's HCM suite is anchored on Employee Central as the core HR system of record, but its stalled consumption at ~$49K/month against a $698K contract means the foundational data layer feeding Talent, Learning, Compensation, and Onboarding is under-populated — every downstream module is starved of the employee, position, and org data it needs to deliver value, which explains the parallel erosion across Performance, Compensation, Career Development, and Learning.
The hire-to-develop talent lifecycle is architecturally contracted but operationally fractured — Recruiting is flatlined at 83% on a static seat pool, Onboarding sits at 12% attainment indicating only a pilot is live, and Career Development is dormant at 15%, meaning new hires are being sourced but never flowed into structured onboarding journeys or skills-based career pathing, breaking the talent continuum SuccessFactors is designed to deliver.
Launch an Employee Central foundation review with Tenneco HR Ops to activate dormant MDF objects, Position Management, and Time Off, and expand employee record coverage so the downstream Talent, Learning, and Performance modules have the core HR data they need to drive consumption.
Rebuild the Recruiting-to-Onboarding-to-Career Development handoff at Tenneco by activating SF Onboarding journeys for the full hiring population and enabling Career Worksheets and Development Plans tied to Learning, closing the $71K Onboarding and $62K Career Development ACV gaps simultaneously.
Solution Area
Supply Chain Management
65.5%
YTD Budget Attainment
YTD Consumed$9K
YTD Budget$14K
YTD ACV: $18K
Projected Consumed: $22K · Projected Budget: $34K · Projected ACV: $43K
Solution Insights and Actions
IBP is architecturally positioned as a unified planning platform spanning demand sensing, inventory optimization, S&OP, and supply response, but the frozen $1,826/month consumption signature indicates Tenneco is exercising only a single planning module — likely baseline demand or forecasting — leaving the multilevel supply planning and collaborative S&OP capabilities dormant.
The flat consumption against a rising budget ($2,757 → $2,848) shows the supply chain planning function has stabilized at a partial-footprint deployment, meaning Tenneco is running disconnected planning silos rather than the integrated commercial-to-supply process IBP was contracted to deliver, with ~$12,281 of FY2026 planning value projected to go unrealized.
Launch an IBP module activation workshop targeting demand sensing, inventory optimization, and S&OP collaboration to convert the dormant capabilities into active planning processes and lift run rate above the $2,848 monthly budget line.
Diagnose the root cause of IBP's frozen $1,826/month ceiling with Tenneco's planning leadership and build a tiered adoption roadmap that sequences response and supply planning onto the existing baseline to close the $12,281 ACV gap.
The Lubrizol Corporation
